data.title: Is Tether moving towards compliance after suspending its $20 billion funding plan?
data.description:
Author: Glendon, Techub News

Today, Bloomberg reported that Tether has pressed the "pause button" on its $20 billion fundraising plan. Previously, Tether had ambitiously sought massive private funding, targeting a valuation of $500 billion, attempting to join the ranks of top private companies such as OpenAI and SpaceX.

Why did Tether suspend this financing plan? It wasn't due to a lack of funding, but rather a strategic adjustment made proactively by Tether. In fact, Tether has a healthy cash flow, with net profits exceeding $10 billion last year, and holds approximately $122 billion in U.S. Treasury bonds, making it the 17th largest holder of U.S. Treasury bonds globally. The core consideration behind Tether's suspension of financing is awaiting the results of its first comprehensive financial audit to address market concerns about its long-standing lack of transparency. For a long time, potential investors and bankers have been urging Tether to increase its financial transparency, and Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino has stated that completing a full audit is a very high priority for the company, planned to be achieved by the end of 2026. On March 24, Tether finally publicly announced that it had signed an agreement with one of the Big Four accounting firms to launch its first-ever complete independent financial statement audit. This audit is considered the largest ever audit in the financial market, covering a complex asset structure including digital assets, traditional reserves, and tokenized liabilities, totaling over $184 billion, marking Tether's official departure from its "black box" operating model. Therefore, while Tether's fundraising process may seem to have been stalled, in reality, market and institutional demands are driving it towards greater compliance. It's worth noting that Tether's announcement of its first comprehensive financial audit is quite coincidental, coinciding with the release of the latest draft of the CLARITY Act, a bill governing the crypto market. The draft clearly defines the boundaries of the stablecoin market: allowing platforms to offer reward programs (such as cashback payments and trading incentives) based on users' stablecoin activities, but prohibiting the payment of users solely for holding stablecoins, while restricting any practice that equates such programs with bank deposits. This provision precisely addresses the core business model differences in the stablecoin market, directly impacting Circle, Tether's biggest rival. While Circle doesn't directly pay interest to users, its business growth heavily relies on paying users high interest rates (approximately 3.5% annualized) through partnerships with platforms like Coinbase to attract funds and expand the circulation of stablecoins. Therefore, the market reacted swiftly after the draft bill was released. Investors worried about the sustainability of its profit model, and coupled with other negative factors, Circle's stock price plummeted by over 20%, marking the company's largest single-day drop since its listing in June 2025. Undoubtedly, the latest draft will have a significant impact on Circle and Coinbase. A complete ban on the "holding coins for interest" model will inevitably weaken Coinbase users' willingness to hold stablecoins, negatively impacting the overall circulation and market demand of USDC, and consequently dragging down Circle's minting volume and reserve interest income. Interestingly, according to sources, while Coinbase and some crypto companies were uncomfortable with the wording of the stablecoin yield section of the CLARITY Act this week, no one has explicitly expressed opposition. This also means that the proposal has a high probability of passing. However, some analysts believe the market reaction is excessive, as the bill does not prohibit Circle from earning interest from its reserves, but only restricts the distribution of yield to users. In contrast, Tether's situation is quite different. Objectively speaking, the latest draft of the US CLARITY Act will have a negligible impact on Tether's USDT. On the one hand, USDT's core business model does not rely on "earning interest by holding tokens." On the other hand, due to its offshore nature and the fundamental mismatch between it and the global regulatory system, USDT already faces multiple structural restrictions on its compliance in the United States. First, Tether is legally registered in El Salvador, but its core operations are located in Hong Kong. The US GENIUS Act explicitly places "foreign issuers" under stricter regulatory frameworks, requiring their reserves to consist 100% of US Treasury bonds and cash, and subject to regulation by the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC). While Tether has significantly increased its US Treasury bond holdings, it still holds high-risk assets such as Bitcoin and gold, contradicting the requirements of the Act. Second, Tether lacks a US domestic financial license and cannot directly access the banking system, relying instead on third parties (such as Cantor Fitzgerald) to hold its US Treasury bonds. In contrast, Circle has received OCC approval to establish a national digital currency bank. This means that Tether cannot be "natively compliant" like Circle; to meet US regulatory requirements, it must undergo a complete corporate restructuring. Fortunately, Tether had already made preparations, launching the regulated stablecoin USAT in the US market in early January of this year. This stablecoin may become a potential beneficiary of the latest draft of the Clarity Act, potentially serving as a key breakthrough in overcoming regulatory barriers. USAT is issued by a newly established US entity of Tether, with 100% of its reserve assets consisting of cash and short-term US Treasury bonds, fully complying with the Clarity Act's definition of a "compliant stablecoin." Like USDT, USAT does not offer "earning interest on holdings," naturally circumventing the Act's restrictions on passive income and eliminating the need to adjust its business model like Circle. This inherently compliant positioning allows it to legally access the US financial system, directly serving institutional investors and retail users in the US. Earlier this month, Tether released its first reserve report on USAT, with Deloitte providing an assurance opinion. This marked the first time one of the Big Four accounting firms had issued an assurance report on stablecoin reserves related to Tether. Now, Tether has launched its first comprehensive audit, which will provide new credibility for USAT's market promotion. Although the audit focuses on USDT reserves, once completed and made public, it will demonstrate Tether's ability to meet international auditing standards, alleviating institutional investors' concerns about its "financial opacity." Based on this, USAT may be able to leverage Tether's influence to provide US institutions with a pathway to access the Tether ecosystem through federal regulatory tools. It should be noted that this bill is currently still in the draft stage, and its final passage and the definition of its specific provisions remain highly uncertain. From a long-term perspective, Circle, having already gone public, undergone an annual audit by Deloitte, and publishes monthly reserve reports, maintains a significant advantage in compliance and transparency. DeFiLlama data shows that, as of this writing, USAT's market capitalization is only $27.75 million, a far cry from USDC. USAT will undoubtedly face numerous challenges if it wants to compete directly with USDC.

Conclusion

Tether's suspension of its fundraising plans, the initiation of a comprehensive audit, and the advancement of the latest draft of the Clarity Act all indicate that the stablecoin market will become increasingly competitive in terms of compliance. Tether's issuance of the USAT reserve report and the initiation of a comprehensive audit of USDT also demonstrate its proactive embrace of compliance. However, Circle, with its first-mover advantage in compliance and its large market base, remains a strong competitor in the compliance race. The competition in the future stablecoin market may no longer be a simple contest of scale, but a comprehensive battle of transparency, compliance, and strategic resilience. The ongoing struggle between Tether and Circle will serve as a microcosm of the sector's compliance transformation.

data.title: As the market crashed, regulators finally provided an answer.
data.description:
Author: Thejaswini M A Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

On March 17th, the SEC and CFTC finally handed over the regulatory rulebook that the crypto industry had been pleading for since 2013. I should have been relieved, and I've been trying to convince myself of this.

Bitcoin is down 44% from its October high. Ethereum is hovering around $2,000, half its value from seven months ago. The total market capitalization of altcoins has evaporated $470 billion from its peak. The Fear & Greed Index is at 11. Not just 11 for a bad week, but 11 out of 100. At this point, people have stopped arguing about where the bottom is and have started selling off everything they have left.

In this very market, on March 17th, the SEC and CFTC released a document finally clarifying what the tokens in your hands actually are. Ten years of litigation, hundreds of enforcement actions, and billions of dollars in legal fees. Countless companies packed their bags and moved to Singapore, unwilling to play guessing games with Jensler any longer. And the answer, ironically, only arrived the week Ethereum fell below $1900. But the reality is: while the token economy is bleeding, the underlying infrastructure is booming. Stablecoin circulation has surpassed $316 billion, and Reality-on-Chain Assets (RWA) have reached $26.5 billion and continue to grow. The momentum is so strong that Morgan Stanley is establishing a crypto trust bank. Meta abandoned Metaverse but plans to integrate stablecoins into WhatsApp. Stripe has processed $400 billion in stablecoin transactions. Nasdaq is building a tokenized equity trading platform. Crypto is becoming the backbone of global finance, and most of the time, it doesn't even need a speculative token. Crypto is no longer just a speculative asset class. The regulatory clarity that arrived on March 17th, tailor-made for first-generation crypto, has arrived in the era of second-generation crypto. But this doesn't mean it's meaningless. SEC Chairman Paul Atkins once said, "We are no longer the Securities and Exchange Commission that regulates everything." Was that statement a little late? This is the first time that US regulators have a unified discourse on crypto assets. Five major categories, all tokens must fall into one of them. I will give you the definitions one by one, just like when you first encounter these concepts.

Digital goods are the main focus. Digital goods are encrypted assets whose value derives from the programmatic operation of a functional cryptographic system, coupled with supply and demand. Their value does not depend on the management and operation of a centralized issuer. If the network is truly decentralized and can operate without the support of a single company, then the asset is a commodity. Jurisdiction lies with the CFTC, not the SEC.

... Sixteen mainstream tokens have been officially listed as digital goods, including: Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, XRP, Cardano, Avalanche, Polkadot, Chainlink, Dogecoin, and Shiba Inu Coin. Dogecoin and Shiba Inu Coin qualify because there is no "pusher" driving their price through their own efforts. No promises, no roadmap, and no continuous work from a team are essential to the token's value. This is why they are considered goods, not securities. The core criterion: whether someone promises you returns based on their work. Digital securities refer to tokenized stocks, bonds, and government bonds. In short, these assets are securities before they are put on the blockchain, and they remain securities afterward. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulates these assets. It's that simple. Digital collectibles are NFTs (Non-Funded Tokens) tied to specific items or experiences. Digital tools are assets used to access software or services without expecting a return on investment. Stablecoins have their own category under the GENIUS Act. Staking, mining, and airdrops are all explicitly legal. The document clearly states that obtaining mining rewards, participating in on-chain staking, and receiving digital commodity airdrops are not considered securities transactions. This removes one of the biggest legal risks hanging over PoS networks since the Gensler era. Packaging non-security tokens is also permitted. The 16 tokens named are all underlying infrastructure tokens that have achieved decentralization for many years. DeFi protocol tokens—JUP, POL, METEOR, and most tokens launched in the last two years—were not included and clearly do not meet the criteria. "A functional crypto system without centralized control" is a very high threshold, which most protocols still under active development do not meet. The gray area that this definition should address remains a gray area for most assets held by ordinary people. Value must come from the programmatic operation of a functional system, not from the promise of an individual. This single standard has transformed a decade-long ambiguity into rules that compliance personnel can truly implement.

But there is a fatal problem

This document does not constitute formal legislation under the Administrative Procedure Act and does not have the binding force of written law or formally promulgated regulations.

This sentence is worth reading again. The 68 pages we have been waiting for are just a regulatory interpretation, not a law, or even a regulation. It is merely an institutional position statement issued by the current chairmen of the SEC and CFTC, which can be revoked at any time.

This interpretation is binding on the two agencies themselves, but in the absence of legislation, future regulatory bodies can amend it. The document itself also reserves the right to refine and expand its interpretation. A future SEC chairman with differing views can overturn it without congressional approval. The next administration doesn't even need new laws; a change in leadership is sufficient. Atkins himself is well aware of this. On the day of its release, he stated his call for Congress to legislate to provide more lasting clarity in the rules. He positioned this interpretation as a transitional measure, awaiting Congress to pass the Comprehensive Market Structure Act. This act is the CLARITY Act, which is currently stuck in the Senate. The House of Representatives passed the CLARITY Act in July 2025 with 294 votes, demonstrating significant bipartisan support and genuine consensus. The bill then entered the Senate, where it subsequently stalled. The core issue holding the bill in motion is stablecoin yields. Banks argue that allowing crypto platforms to pay interest on stablecoin balances will trigger deposit outflows—people will withdraw money from savings accounts and exchange it for USDC in hopes of higher returns. Bank lobbying groups acted swiftly. The Senate Banking Committee cancelled its scheduled consideration of the provisions in January 2026. The bill made no progress for two months. On March 20, Senators Tom Tillis and Angela Althorkbrooks confirmed an agreement in principle regarding stablecoin rewards, which received support from the White House. The agreement prohibits passive yields on stablecoins; rewards linked to payments and platform usage remain permitted. Neither side was satisfied, and compromises often result from this. However, the yield agreement is just one of five things that need to be completed before the CLARITY Act can take effect. The remaining four legislative steps are being completed during one of the most stressful periods of the year. The bill will undergo review by the Senate Banking Committee and a full Senate vote (requiring 60 votes). The Banking Committee's review is scheduled for the second half of April, after the Easter recess. Senator Bernie Moreno warned that if the bill fails to reach the full Senate before May, digital asset legislation may not progress for years. Furthermore, the Iran issue consumed Senate time, Trump demanded priority passage of the voter ID bill, DeFi-related provisions remained unresolved (Senate Democrats raised concerns about illicit finance), and ethical provisions were unresolved (especially whether high-ranking officials should be prohibited from personally profiting from crypto assets; given the current administration's crypto holdings, this issue is highly politically sensitive). Senate Republicans were also discussing including deregulation of community banks in the bill as a political bargaining chip, leading to a whole new set of negotiations. The House Financial Services Committee recently held a hearing entitled "Tokenization and the Future of Securities: Modernizing Capital Markets." Witnesses included representatives from SIFMA, the Blockchain Association, DTCC, and Nasdaq. Both Nasdaq and the NYSE are building tokenized equity venues. DTCC controls current clearing and settlement. Once DTCC acknowledges the efficiency of blockchain, the related debate will essentially be over. Thus, the current situation is: infrastructure is being rapidly built on a set of rules that may not exist in two years. This is the core contradiction in the industry right now. Companies are making multi-billion dollar decisions, building custody systems, tokenization platforms, and staking infrastructure, all based on a regulatory interpretation that is persuasive but lacks legal binding force. Which are permanent, and which are not? If you hold any of these 16 named tokens—ETH, SOL, XRP, etc.—they are officially classified as digital goods under current US rules because two regulatory officials have stated this. This classification will remain in effect as long as the current or successor officials approve it. If the CLARITY Act passes, it will become law. No future president can bypass Congress to overturn it. The named assets will receive permanent status, and the classification system will be legally binding. If the bill fails to pass before May, this classification system will be based solely on the views of one administration. The 16 named assets are temporarily safe, but everything not named is not. The vast majority of DeFi, the vast majority of new tokens, and any permissionless asset without a clearly defined issuer remain in a gray area not explicitly covered by the rules. That much-anticipated conclusion was written in pencil. Someone needs to pick up a pen and formally implement it. Everything depends on the Senate's outcome over the next six weeks. Will these rules last until they truly take effect?

data.title: Polymarket bids farewell to the era of zero commissions: What does the fee reform mean?
data.description:
On March 26, prediction market platform Polymarket announced a major policy change: starting March 30, it will begin charging a Taker fee for almost all trading categories. This means that in a few days, the era of betting on Polymarket without paying more than the amount wagered will end.

I. Comparison of Polymarket Fee Adjustment Before and After

Polymarket details the fees issue in its official documentation.

Polymarket charges a small transaction fee for certain markets. These fees fund a market maker rebate program, which redistributes a portion of the fees daily to market makers to incentivize them to provide more liquidity and narrow spreads.

Geopolitical and world events markets are fee-free. Polymarket does not collect any fees or profits from trading activity in these markets.

Furthermore, Polymarket does not charge fees for USDC deposits or withdrawals (but intermediaries such as Coinbase or MoonPay may charge their own fees). 1. Current Fee Schedule Currently, only the cryptocurrency and sports betting markets have implemented taker fees.

2. New Rates

Starting March 30, 2026, the fee parameters will be expanded to cover more market categories and will adopt updated rates.

... Fees are calculated using the following formula: fee = C × p × feeRate × (p × (1 - p))^exponent Where C = number of shares traded, p = share price. Fee parameters vary by market category: [Image of a PNG file] Transaction fees are calculated in USDC and vary based on share price. However, buy orders are charged per share, while sell orders are charged in USDC. The actual rate peaks at a 50% probability and decreases symmetrically towards both ends.

Transaction fees are rounded to four decimal places. The minimum transaction fee is 0.0001 USDC. Any transaction smaller than this amount is rounded to zero, so extremely small transactions close to this value may incur no fees at all.

Transaction fees are highest when the contract price is close to 50/50, as this represents the greatest uncertainty. Once the outcome becomes clearer, the transaction fee gradually drops to zero. Therefore, transaction fees for contracts priced at $0.02 or $0.98 are almost negligible. However, any contract priced at $0.50 will reach its peak transaction fee.

Fees vary by category. Cryptocurrency fees are the highest, reaching 1.80%. Economic fees are approximately 1.50%, Culture and Weather fees are 1.25%, Politics fees are 1%, and Sports fees are the lowest at 0.75%.

3. API User Fee Processing

Using the SDK

The official CLOB client automatically processes fees—it retrieves the fee rate and includes it in the signed order payload.

Automatic client actions:

  1. Retrieve the fee rate for the market token ID;

  2. Include feeRateBps in the order structure;

  3. Sign the order containing the fee.

No additional action is required. Orders also function correctly in fee-enabled markets.

Using the REST API If you are calling the REST API directly or building your own order signature, you must manually include the fee rate in the payload of the signed order. Step 1: Before creating the order, obtain the fee rate for the token ID; Step 2: Add this field to your order object. This value is part of the signature payload—CLOB uses this value to verify your signature. Step 3: Sign and Submit: Before signing, include the order object in the feeRateBps file; Sign the complete order; Send a POST request to the order endpoint. II. How much revenue does Polymarket generate from transaction fees?

1. Polymarket's Revenue Changes

According to a joint report by Keyrock and Dune, in 2025, Polymarket's trading volume reached $21.5 billion; it processed 95 million transactions (approximately 54% of the cumulative trading volume), and its monthly trading volume increased from approximately 45,000 transactions to approximately 19 million transactions (a 421-fold increase).

According to data compiled by an exchange on Dune, since some markets began charging trading fees on January 6, Polymarket has accumulated approximately $7.94 million in fee revenue, of which approximately $4.99 million was in February. (Markets where Polymarket charges fees include "15-minute cryptocurrency price fluctuations" and sports markets such as Serie A and the NCAA.)

In the past 30 days, Polymarket's trading volume was approximately $9.55 billion.

Based on current trading volume levels and assuming an effective composite commission rate, this translates to approximately $25 million in monthly revenue, $300 million annually, or $833,000 per day.

2. Polymarket's Valuation Changes

Analysts believe that current revenue levels will help maintain Polymarket's valuation, which is now close to $20 billion. Just a year ago, its valuation was approximately $1.2 billion.

Specifically, according to data disclosed by Polymarket CEO Shayne Coplan: in 2024, when Blockchain Capital led a $55 million funding round, the company's valuation was $350 million; in 2025, when Founders Fund led a $150 million funding round, the company's valuation was $1.2 billion; in October of the same year, Intercontinental Exchange Group invested $2 billion in Polymarket, valuing the company at approximately $9 billion post-investment; at the end of October, Polymarket held preliminary talks with investors regarding a new investment with a valuation between $12 billion and $15 billion. According to the latest data from PM Insights: as of January 19, 2026, Polymarket's implied valuation reached $11.6 billion. 3. Changes in Polymarket's Price-to-Sales Ratio (PS Ratio) Based on February's fee revenue of approximately $4.99 million, current annual revenue is $59.88 million, and the current valuation is $11.6 billion. According to Polymarket's future fee structure, annual revenue will be around $300 million. Assuming the valuation remains unchanged, the PS ratio (price-to-sales ratio) has changed from 193.7 to 38.7. (PS ratio = Company market capitalization ÷ Company revenue, i.e., "how much the market is willing to pay for every dollar of revenue.") At the current PS ratio of 193.7, Polymarket's future depends on explosive user growth, a monopoly in the prediction market, and becoming a global information pricing infrastructure. However, given the challenges of regulation and user stickiness, can Polymarket truly support a PS ratio of 193.7? In the future, when revenue reaches $300 million, Polymarket's valuation will at least appear more reasonable. Polymarket will gradually shift from inflated valuations based on storytelling to valuations supported by real revenue. III. Will Polymarket's adjustments affect user stickiness? The new fees from Polymarket will also be used for structural updates. The Maker Rebate Program will return collected fees to liquidity providers in the form of daily USDC payments. This move aims to deepen the market and reduce spreads. For example, political trading market makers will receive a 25% rebate on fees collected in their category. This mechanism should create a healthier ecosystem because market makers will publish more accurate quotes, traders will pay less, and overall liquidity will improve. If user numbers remain stable after the fee policy is implemented, Polymarket can successfully convert free users into paying users without churning. Conversely, a significant drop in user numbers will be detrimental to Polymarket's development. However, it seems that users will continue to use Polymarket and will not abandon it because of the fees.

data.title: Lin Junyang's first post after leaving Alibaba: The era of intelligent agents is coming.
data.description:
Author: Lin Junyang, former head of Tongyi Qianwen Qwen, Alibaba's youngest P10. He will leave Alibaba in March 2026.

Original table title: From "Reasoning" Thinking toFrom "Reasoning" Thinking to "Agentic" Thinking The past two years have reshaped how we evaluate models and what we expect from them. OpenAI's o1 proved that "thinking" can be a first-class capability—one that you can specifically train for and make available to users. DeepSeek-R1 demonstrated that this style of reasoning can be replicated and extended beyond the original top-tier labs. OpenAI describes o1 as a model trained using reinforcement learning that "thinks before answering"; while DeepSeek positions R1 as an open-source reasoning model that can directly compete with o1. That phase was significant. However, the focus in the first half of 2025 was primarily on "reasoning thinking": how to allocate more computational power to models during inference periods, how to train them with stronger reward signals, and how to present or control this additional inference input. The question now is, what's next? I believe the answer is "agentic thinking": thinking for action, constantly updating plans based on feedback from the real world during interaction with the environment. 1. What did the rise of O1 and R1 truly teach us? The first wave of inference models taught us that if we want to extend reinforcement learning (RL) in language models, we need deterministic, stable, and scalable feedback signals. Verifiable domains such as mathematics, code, and logic became core because... This is because the reward signals in these scenarios are far stronger than regular preference supervision. They allow reinforcement learning to optimize for "correctness" rather than "seemingly reasonableness." Infrastructure then becomes paramount. Once the model is trained to infer over longer trajectories, reinforcement learning is no longer just a lightweight add-on to supervised fine-tuning (SFT). It becomes a complex systems problem. You need large-scale policy rollouts, high-throughput validation mechanisms, stable policy updates, and efficient sampling capabilities. The emergence of inference models represents both a breakthrough in modeling capabilities and a victory for infrastructure engineering. OpenAI describes O1 as an inference product line trained using RL, and DeepSeek R1 subsequently further confirms this direction. It demonstrated to the world the immense amount of dedicated algorithms and infrastructure work required for reasoning-based RL. This marked the industry's first major shift: from extending pre-training to extending post-training to enhance reasoning capabilities. 2. The real problem is never just "integrating thinking and instructions." In early 2025, many on our Qwen team had a grand vision: the ideal system should unify the "thinking" and "instruction" modes. It would support adjustable reasoning intensity, mentally similar to "low/medium/high" reasoning settings. Even better, it could automatically infer the required amount of reasoning based on prompts and context, thus deciding when to answer immediately, when to think longer, and when to invest massive computational resources in truly challenging problems. Conceptually, this is the right direction. Qwen3 is one of the clearest public attempts. It introduces a "hybrid thinking mode," balancing thinking and non-thinking behaviors within the same model family, emphasizing a controllable thinking budget, and describing a four-stage post-training pipeline—which explicitly includes "thinking mode fusion" after long-CoT (long-CoT) cold start and inference RL. However, fusion is easier said than done. The difficulty lies in the data. When people talk about fusion of thinking and instructions, the first thing that comes to mind is often model-side compatibility: Can a checkpoint support two modes? Can a chat template seamlessly switch between the two? Can the service stack provide corresponding control switches? However, the deeper contradiction lies in the fundamental difference in data distribution and behavioral objectives between these two models. In attempting to balance "model fusion" with "improving the quality and diversity of post-training data," we encountered some pitfalls. During the review process, we closely observed how users actually used the thinking and instruction models in real-world scenarios. A powerful instruction model's rewards often come from directness, conciseness, adherence to format, and maintaining extremely low latency on repetitive, high-volume enterprise-level tasks (such as rewriting, annotation, template support, structured extraction, and operational Q&A). Conversely, a powerful thinking model's rewards come from consuming more tokens on challenging problems, maintaining the coherence of internal logical structures, exploring alternative paths, and reserving sufficient internal computation to substantially improve the final accuracy. These two behavioral patterns are mutually restrictive. If the merged data is not carefully planned, the result is often a lose-lose situation: the "thinking" behavior becomes noisy, bloated, or indecisive; while the "instruction" behavior loses its crispness, reliability decreases, and the cost of use far exceeds the actual expectations of business users. Therefore, in practice, separating the two remains attractive. Later in 2025, following the initial hybrid architecture of Qwen3, the 2507 product line released distinct Instruct and Thinking updates, including separate 30B and 235B variants. In commercial deployments, many customers still crave high-throughput, low-cost, and highly controllable instruction behavior for batch operations. In these scenarios, fusion does not bring significant benefits. Separating the two product lines... This allowed the team to more purely address the data and training challenges specific to each mode. Other labs took the opposite approach. Anthropic openly advocated for an integrated model philosophy: Claude 3.7 Sonnet was positioned as a hybrid inference model, allowing users to choose between regular responses or extended thinking, and API users could also set a thinking budget. Anthropic explicitly stated that they believed inference should be a built-in integrated capability, not a separate, independent model. GLM-4.5 similarly touted itself as a hybrid inference model combining two modes, attempting to integrate inference, coding, and agent capabilities; DeepSeek later followed suit with V3.1's "thinking and non-thinking" hybrid inference mechanism. The core issue here is whether this integration is natural and organic. If thinking and instructions are merely forcibly crammed into the same model weights... If they behave like two clumsily stitched-together independent personalities, the product experience will still feel very awkward. Truly successful integration requires a smooth range of inference engagement. The model should be able to express different levels of engagement and adaptively make choices under ideal conditions. GPT-style engagement control precisely illustrates this point: it's a strategy for allocating computational power, not a simple binary switch. 3. Why Anthropic's Direction is a Beneficial Correction Anthropic's public relations efforts regarding the release of Claude 3.7 and Claude 4 were quite restrained. They focused on integrated inference, user-controlled thinking budgets, real-world tasks, code quality, and the later-added ability to invoke tools during extended thinking. Claude 3.7 was presented as a budget-controlled hybrid inference model; Claude Step 4 goes a step further, allowing inference processes to intertwine with tool calls. Meanwhile, Anthropic repeatedly emphasizes that coding, long-running tasks, and proxy workflows are their core objectives. Simply generating longer inference paths doesn't automatically make the model smarter. In many cases, excessively exposed inference processes reveal inefficient resource allocation. If a model attempts to reason everything in the same verbose manner, it indicates a failure to prioritize, streamline information, or take any action. Anthropic's development trajectory conveys a more disciplined perspective: thinking should be shaped by the target workload. If the goal is coding, then the value of thinking should lie in codebase navigation, planning, task decomposition, error recovery, and tool orchestration. If the goal is proxy workflows, then thinking should focus on improving execution quality over long periods, rather than writing flowery interim statements. This emphasis on "goal utility" points to a grander trend: we are moving from an era focused on training models to an era focused on training agents. We also clearly pointed this out in our Qwen3 blog—"We are transitioning from an era focused on training models to an era centered on training agents," and linking the future breakthrough point of RL to the environmental feedback required for long-term inference. An agent is a system capable of making plans, deciding when to act, calling on tools, perceiving environmental feedback, adjusting strategies, and operating continuously over long periods. Its essential definition lies in its closed-loop interaction with the real world. 4. What does "agent-based thinking" truly mean? The proxy mindset is a completely different optimization goal. The standard for evaluating "reasoning-based thinking" is usually the quality of internal deliberation before arriving at the final answer: whether the model can solve theorems, write proofs, generate bug-free code, or pass benchmark tests. The standard for evaluating "proxy mindset," however, is whether the model can continuously make substantial progress in its interaction with the environment. The core question shifts from "Does the model think long enough?" to "Is the way the model thinks sufficient to support effective action?" Proxy mindset must address several problems that pure reasoning models can largely avoid: a. Deciding when to stop thinking and take action. b. Choosing which tool to use and the order in which they are used. c. Integrating noisy or incomplete observations from the environment. d. Readjusting the plan after encountering failure. e. Maintaining logical coherence in multiple rounds of dialogue and multiple tool calls. In short, a model with proxy thinking must reason through action. 5. Why is the infrastructure for surrogate-based reinforcement learning more challenging? Once the goal shifts from "solving benchmark problems" to "completing interactive tasks," the technology stack of RL undergoes a dramatic change. The infrastructure used in traditional inference RL is far from sufficient. In inference RL, you can usually treat policy rollouts as relatively independent trajectories, equipped with clear and explicit evaluators. However, in surrogate-based RL, the policy is deeply embedded within a massive supporting framework: tool servers, browsers, terminals, search engines, simulators, execution sandboxes, API layers, memory systems, and orchestration frameworks. The environment is no longer a static referee; it becomes an integral part of the entire training system. This has given rise to a completely new system-level requirement: training and inference must be more thoroughly decoupled. Without this decoupling, the throughput of policy unfolding will collapse directly. Imagine a coding agent that must run its generated code in a real-time testing framework: the inference end will be forced to pause while waiting for execution feedback, and the training end will starve due to not receiving complete trajectory data. The GPU utilization of the entire pipeline will be far lower than that of traditional inference RL. If we add factors such as tool latency, local observability, and stateful environments, these inefficiencies will be further amplified. As a result, the progress of the entire experiment will become extremely slow and painful long before you reach the expected performance metrics. The environment itself has thus become a core research product. In the era of SFT (Supervised Fine-tuning), we once flocked to the diversity of data. But in the agent era, We should relentlessly pursue the quality of the environment: stability, realism, scene coverage, difficulty levels, state diversity, richness of feedback, anti-cheating capabilities, and scalability of strategy development. Building virtual environments has become a truly hardcore entrepreneurial track, no longer just a side project. If agents are destined to be trained in production-like conditions, then the environment itself is part of the core capability technology stack. 6. The Next Cutting-Edge Area: More Practical Thinking Skills My personal expectation is that agent-based thinking will become the dominant form of thinking in the future. I believe it will eventually eliminate most of the old "static monologue" reasoning thinking—that is, the overly verbose, isolated, and closed internal trajectory that tries to cover up its lack of interactive capabilities by uttering more and more text. Even when facing extremely difficult mathematical or coding tasks, A truly advanced system should also have the right to search, simulate, run, check, verify, and modify. Our ultimate goal is to solve real-world problems robustly and efficiently. The biggest pain point in training such systems lies in "reward hacking." Once a model gains substantial tool access, reward hacking becomes extremely destructive. A model with search functionality might learn to search for answers online directly during RL training. A coding agent might exploit unpublished future information in the codebase, abuse logs, or find shortcuts to directly disable the task. An environment with hidden vulnerabilities can make a model's strategy appear extraordinary, but in reality, it's just training a cheating expert. Compared to the inference era, the situation in the agent era is far more delicate and dangerous. More powerful tools make models more useful, but they also amplify the attack surface of spurious optimizations many times over. We can fully foresee... The next major academic bottleneck will emerge in environmental design, the robustness of evaluators, anti-cheating protocols, and establishing more standardized interface standards between policies and the physical world. Despite the numerous difficulties, the general direction remains unshakable: tool-enabled thinking is inherently more valuable and more likely to bring about a real leap in productivity than thinking in isolation. Agent-based thinking also signifies the rise of "harness engineering." Future core intelligence will increasingly rely on the collaborative organization of multiple agents: a central orchestrator responsible for planning and scheduling tasks, specialized agents acting as domain experts, and sub-agents responsible for executing vertically segmented tasks (they not only do the work but also help control the context, avoid memory contamination, and maintain physical isolation between different levels of thinking). The future of industry is shifting from training models to training agents. And ultimately, this leads to training massive systems. The first phase of the inference wave established an ironclad rule: as long as the feedback signals are reliable enough and the infrastructure can support it, layering reinforcement learning on top of language models can give rise to qualitatively transformative cognitive abilities. A more profound industry leap is shifting from "reasoning-based thinking" to "agent-based thinking": that is, from simply thinking a little longer to thinking in order to take action. The core target of training has shifted. It is no longer just the model itself, but a symbiotic system of "model + environment," more specifically, the agent and its surrounding supporting framework. This completely overturns our understanding of "core research products": while model architecture and training data are important, However, environmental design, the infrastructure for strategy deployment, the anti-interference capability of the evaluator, and the underlying interface for multi-agent collaboration will be elevated to an equal or even higher status. It also redefines what constitutes "good thinking": true "good" thinking refers to the thought process that most effectively supports action under the constraints of the real world, rather than simply competing on who generates the longest text or whose computational process is the most conspicuous. This also changes the logic of future business competition's moats. In the inference era, whoever had a better RL algorithm, purer feedback signals, and a more scalable training pipeline would win. In the agent era, the killer feature will become whoever possesses a more realistic environment, a smoother "training-integrated" architecture, stronger framework engineering capabilities, and who can most perfectly balance the "model's decision" with the "real consequences of that decision." Close that crucial feedback loop.

data.title: OpenAI loses to a thousand questions: a cash register can't grow out of the chat box.
data.description:
Author: Kaori

In March 2026, OpenAI officially announced the abandonment of Instant Checkout.

Just six months ago, this feature was touted by Silicon Valley media as a crucial function laying the foundation for the future of Agentic Commerce. Sam Altman even described it on multiple occasions as a very important money-printing machine for OpenAI's future. Now, it has died from almost zero real transaction conversion.

Interestingly, on the other side of the Pacific, Alibaba's AI shopping feature, Qianwen, has already been fully opened for testing. You can say "a cup of milk tea" in the chat box, and the delivery person will knock on your door half an hour later.

Both are AI, both are chatbots, but one is a castle in the air that fades away in disgrace, while the other is a powerful force in consumer spending. The same path, drastically different results—the difference isn't because the model isn't smart enough. When we talk about AI shopping, what are we really talking about? In the second half of 2025, this was the hottest sector. ChatGPT had 900 million weekly active users; even if only one ten-thousandth of them made a purchase during a conversation, the revenue would be staggering. But in the six months from the high-profile launch of instant checkout to its cancellation, many cruel stories unfolded. "You can't run a supermarket without owning shelves." Let's rewind to September 2025, the day OpenAI announced instant checkout. The entire retail industry was ecstatic. Shopify President Harley Finkelstein called it the new frontier of online retail. On the day of the announcement, Etsy's stock price jumped 16%. Partners quickly materialized, and Etsy allowed US sellers' products to appear directly on ChatGPT, even covering transaction fees to expedite service launch. Walmart listed approximately 200,000 items. PayPal planned to integrate its wallet with ChatGPT checkout as part of the partnership, and also pledged to increase its purchases of OpenAI's API and enterprise ChatGPT subscriptions. Stripe and OpenAI jointly developed the Agentic Commerce Protocol, attempting to establish an industry standard for AI-assisted transactions. But six months later, the bubble burst. OpenAI had promised to integrate with over 1 million Shopify merchants, but only a paltry 30 or so were actually online. The landing page Shopify created specifically for ChatGPT now quietly redirects back to its official website homepage. Even more concerning, OpenAI's internal data shows that while many users browse and compare prices in ChatGPT, almost no one actually places an order through the chat interface. According to Walmart's data, the conversion rate for those who return to the retailer's official website to checkout is three times higher than those who remain in ChatGPT. Forrester's research confirms this; among frequent users of AI question-answering engines, making purchases within the engine is the lowest adoption rate. Why doesn't buying things in ChatGPT work? Because OpenAI is attempting to function as an e-commerce platform without owning any commercial infrastructure. The most superficial reason is habit. People use ChatGPT like they use Google—to search for information and make comparisons. Once they've chosen, they go to a trusted place to swipe their card. Asking users to enter their credit card number in an interface meant for ChatGPT inherently carries a sense of insecurity. Users are willing to let AI help them choose skincare products, but when it comes to actually paying, the cold, impersonal dialog box doesn't provide the reassurance they crave. Moreover, even if users were willing to spend money, OpenAI couldn't handle it. As of February 2026, OpenAI hadn't even built a system for collecting and paying sales tax across US states. This is infrastructure that Amazon and eBay spent years building. Not to mention fraud detection, returns processing, and consumer protection compliance. Ensuring real-time accuracy of price, inventory, and shipping information for millions of products isn't just about writing a few lines of elegant code; it's a quagmire. Forrester analysts pointed out that instant checkout was plagued with errors during its launch, lacked support for multi-item carts, had no promotional codes, and even shipping information was opaque. The most awkward situation isn't for OpenAI itself, but for the partners it was brought in. PayPal not only invested engineering resources in integration but also pledged to increase its purchases of OpenAI's APIs and enterprise subscriptions. Now, the returns on the shopping side have evaporated, but the purchase commitments remain. According to sources, PayPal and OpenAI are evaluating how to continue their relationship. Etsy's situation isn't easy either. It previously subsidized transaction fees for merchants, and now it has to build its own ChatGPT app from scratch, without even agreeing on a fee structure. An Etsy spokesperson said it's unclear even whether OpenAI will charge for in-app transactions. Stripe is in a slightly better position because it already handles OpenAI's consumer subscription payments, a revenue stream that doesn't rely on shopping features. But most partners don't have this buffer. For a company that is fully shifting its focus to enterprise clients, this constantly changing cooperation model is a huge hidden danger. Alibaba achieved this, but it was both a gift and a constraint from its ecosystem. On January 15, 2026, while OpenAI was still struggling with instant checkout, something happened on the other side of the world. Alibaba held a press conference in Hangzhou, announcing that the Qianwen App was fully integrated with Taobao, Alipay, Taobao Flash Sale, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps. At the press conference, Wu Jia, president of Qianwen's C-end Business Group, said to his phone, "Order me 40 cups of Bawangchaji's 'Bo Ya Jue Xian' tea," and the rider delivered them half an hour later. If we compare OpenAI's instant checkout and Qianwen's AI shopping, Qianwen's success isn't due to whose big model is smarter, but rather who owns the entire chain from finding products to receiving packages. Taobao's hundreds of millions of SKUs, Alipay's native payment system, and the logistics networks of STO Express, YTO Express, ZTO Express, and Yunda Express are readily available. When you say in Qianwen, "I'm going hiking in Siguniang Mountain next week, what equipment do I need?", it can directly pull up a list. You just click on the card to place the order—it's incredibly smooth because there are no cross-company data authorization negotiations or profit-sharing disputes involved. This is why domestic companies like DeepSeek and Kimi can't do this. No matter how powerful the reasoning ability or how robust the long text, without a product shelf and payment, all they can do is throw a link at you. Alibaba has integrated the product shelf into the chat box, while OpenAI is trying to make the chat box pretend to be a product shelf. But is this really a perfect final form? Alibaba succeeded because it forcefully incorporated its extremely cumbersome commercial ecosystem into a large model. However, when Qianwen acts as both referee and player, can its recommendations remain objective? If I ask Qianwen which phone is good, will it prioritize recommending a product from Taobao due to commercial interests? When an AI loses its neutral search stance and becomes a super shopping guide for its own e-commerce platform, can it still be considered a general-purpose brain? Essentially, this uses a cumbersome old ecosystem to hijack new technological entry points. Qianwen's "success" is not only a gift from the ecosystem but also a shackle. $50 Billion and the Elephant in the Room OpenAI also realized it lacked this cumbersome ecosystem, but that wasn't the whole reason for its abandonment. The real elephant in the room is Amazon. At the end of February, Amazon announced a $50 billion investment in OpenAI, becoming the exclusive third-party cloud provider for its enterprise platform, Frontier. When your biggest benefactor is a giant that holds 40% of the US e-commerce market share and is aggressively promoting its AI-powered shopping assistant, continuing to add a checkout function within your app makes you seem incredibly inconsiderate. Moreover, this sum of money is itself a powder keg. Microsoft believes that AWS's hosting of the Frontier platform violates its exclusive cloud agreement with OpenAI and is considering legal action. OpenAI's lawyers are using technical terms like "stateful architecture" to circumvent the spirit of the contract. To survive in the competition among giants, OpenAI must make trade-offs. In mid-March, OpenAI CEO Fidji Simo announced a major strategic shift at an all-hands meeting: "We cannot miss this moment because of side projects." What prompted OpenAI's urgency was Anthropic's rapid rise in the enterprise market. Claude Code and Cowork products have made Anthropic the top choice for enterprise customers. Ramp credit card data shows that new enterprise customers choose Anthropic three times more often than OpenAI. OpenAI expanded too rapidly last year: Sora video generation, the Atlas browser, Jony Ive hardware devices, e-commerce features, advertising, and an adult mode. Now, they must narrow their focus to two core battlegrounds: coding tools and enterprise customers. After all, making money from enterprise customers is much more reliable than squeezing a few percentage points from transaction fees in chat windows. OpenAI expects enterprise customers to contribute half of its total revenue this year, up from the current approximately 40%. To this end, it plans to double its workforce from 4,500 to 8,000, with the new employees focusing on engineering, research, sales, and product development. In San Francisco, OpenAI signed a new lease, expanding its office space to over 1 million square feet. The real battleground for AI shopping isn't at the checkout counter. OpenAI's retreat doesn't mean AI shopping is dead; on the contrary, the upper half of the funnel has been completely reshaped. More than half of American consumers are already accustomed to letting AI make decisions for them. Instead of searching for floor scrubbers and scrolling through ten pages of ads, they directly ask, "Which one offers the best value?" Discovery, research, and comparison—these actions are all being moved forward, and the value of retailers' own channels is being eroded at an accelerated pace. However, the last mile of the transaction loop requires not a smarter model, but a more complete infrastructure. OpenAI has clearly stated that it will prioritize product search and discovery, and add advertising to ChatGPT. This is its way of monetizing at the discovery layer, which is much more realistic than building its own checkout. Finally, the most likely entity in the US to replicate Alibaba's path is not OpenAI, but Amazon. It possesses user profiles, product graphs, payment channels, and fulfillment infrastructure. OpenAI failed in its attempt to build its own e-commerce loop, then took money from the largest e-commerce platform, and may ultimately become a traffic portal for that platform. In China, Alibaba's full-stack advantage led Qianwen down a different path, but currently, only Alibaba has been able to succeed on this path. Qianwen's president, Wu Jia, made a crucial statement: "Comprehensive Agents are extremely competitive, while vertical Agents are increasingly proving to be a phase-specific product. In the future, there won't be many AI applications that can stand alone as entry points." In business terms, this means that those platforms that already possess a complete ecosystem will be able to create a closed-loop AI shopping experience, not AI companies that build everything from scratch. A chat window can't grow into a cash register, but if the cash register is already in your store, placing a chatty AI next to it would seem perfectly natural. This is the most important lesson for AI shopping in 2026.

data.title: Revolut: $6 billion in revenue, 70 million users, and large-scale banking services.
data.description:
Author: insights4vc, Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

Few privately held fintech companies have combined scale, growth, and profitability as well as Revolut. With over 70 million customers, a continuously expanding balance sheet, and an increasingly diversified revenue structure, the company is increasingly resembling a global digital bank rather than a traditional innovative fintech company.

In this report, we will analyze Revolut's development history, the advantages and constraints of its existing model, and the role of digital assets in its overall strategic direction.

；

Revolut 2025 Key Metrics

Summary

  • Scale: Revolut currently serves over 70 million individual customers and approximately 80 The company has 10,000 corporate clients, representing year-on-year growth of 30% and 33% respectively in 2025. Revenue reached $6 billion in 2025 (a 46% year-on-year increase), with pre-tax profit of $2.3 billion (a 57% year-on-year increase). Total client funds balance grew by 66% to $67.5 billion, exceeding the growth rate of the number of clients. Business Diversification: All 11 core product lines generated annual revenue exceeding $135 million, with a relatively balanced structure of subscription fees, payment fees, foreign exchange, wealth management, and interest income. Notably, Revolut Business, the corporate banking business, contributed approximately 16% of total revenue. The credit portfolio grew by 120% to $2.9 billion in 2025, with interest income ($974 million) increasing by 23% year-on-year as the credit portfolio expanded. While transaction fees (bank cards, foreign exchange, etc.) still account for the largest share, these changes are driving Revolut towards a more stable interest-based revenue structure. Customer Activity: Product innovation and network effects continue to drive high user stickiness. In Europe, approximately one-fifth of the working-age population uses Revolut. 16 million new users were added in 2025 (total users reached 68.3 million in December 2025), with 63% of new individual customers coming from organic referrals. Paid subscriptions increased by 42% year-over-year, and RevPoints loyalty program users surged from 6.6 million to 17 million, demonstrating the effectiveness of cross-selling. Data indicates that Revolut is increasing the share of wallets per user, rather than simply opening one-time accounts. Strategic Advantages: Revolut's core competitiveness includes a comprehensive product portfolio (banking, payments, wealth management, and crypto assets) and an agile technology platform. The company has achieved economies of scale in key markets such as the UK and Europe, and has deeply engaged users through the launch of new services such as mortgages and mobile communications. Low-cost digital operations and 24/7 online customer service effectively control costs, significantly improving operating leverage—the pre-tax profit margin reached 38% in 2025, higher than 35% in 2024, supporting its continued expansion. Management has repeatedly emphasized that a "diversified, highly resilient business model" is the core driver of record-breaking performance. Key Risks: Rapid growth is accompanied by regulatory and enforcement risks. In 2025, EU regulators fined Revolut's EU branch €3.5 million for anti-money laundering compliance loopholes; UK regulators launched a review of its cross-border risk control system, leading to a delay in the issuance of a full UK banking license. The average account balance per customer remains low, with only about 45% of users using Revolut as their primary account. Deposit funds and user retention capabilities need improvement. Analysts acknowledge that transaction fees (foreign exchange, credit card spending) and crypto asset-related revenue, which rely on market conditions, are cyclical, and sustainable profitability depends on rapid user and product growth. The acquisition of new qualifications such as a US banking license and the large-scale expansion of the lending business have not yet been fully validated. Crypto Asset Business: Revolut's crypto strategy is an integral part of its overall business, but not its core. The company launched its cryptocurrency trading service as early as 2017 and subsequently built a regulated digital asset platform (which has obtained an EU CASP license). Currently, it offers custodial crypto trading services (the Revolut X platform supports over 250 tokens) and advanced features such as zero-fee order placement and API access. However, crypto trading is only one of the wealth investment product lines (alongside stocks, ETFs, and staking), and management did not highlight it separately in the 2025 results. Analysis suggests that the current crypto business contributes limited to total revenue, primarily serving as a tool for user activation and engagement. Recent participation in the UK Financial Conduct Authority's sandbox experiment (a pilot program for stablecoins in the British pound) may indicate future innovation directions. Overall, crypto assets are a strategic growth area for Revolut, strengthening its super-application positioning and building a regulated digital financial bridge. However, this also comes with volatility and regulatory risks, positioning it as a supplement to, rather than a replacement for, its core banking business.

Revolut is one of the fastest growing financial institutions (Source: Revolut 2025 Annual Report, a16z analysis)

Strategic Overview

  • Established / Headquarters: 2015

Founded / Headquarters: 2015

Revolut's Development History

Revolut started in 2015 as a multi-currency prepaid debit card for travelers and expatriates, providing real-time foreign exchange services at interbank rates, solving the common pain point of users being troubled by high exchange fees. The company experienced rapid growth in its early stages, reaching approximately 300,000 users by 2016. Starting in 2017, Revolut significantly added new features, such as peer-to-peer payments, budget management tools, and paid subscription packages (Premium/Metal Edition), to increase user activity and generate recurring revenue. Crucially, Revolut launched cryptocurrency trading functionality for users at the end of 2017, directly supporting Bitcoin, Litecoin, and Ethereum trading within the app. This was a pioneering move among fintech companies, thus initiating a diversified revenue stream.

Revolut 2015-2020, data source: Whitesight

A significant strategic milestone was obtaining a European banking license through a Lithuanian bank at the end of 2018—Revolut was one of the first fintech companies to achieve this. This enabled it to hold customer deposits within the EU and offer IBAN accounts, supporting its ambitions to expand beyond pure electronic money business.

With its licenses secured, Revolut accelerated its expansion into new markets (Asia, the US, and Latin America) and added various banking features. For example, the company launched Revolut Business for corporate accounts in 2017, partnered with DriveWealth around 2019 to launch stock trading services, and launched zero-commission ETFs and other digital wealth management products in the European Economic Area in 2022-2023. Through continuous iteration (usually in the form of mobile app updates), Revolut maintained strong organic growth; in fact, most of its growth during 2020-2021 came from word-of-mouth. Regulatory landscape has profoundly shaped its development trajectory. As a company originating in the UK, Revolut shifted its services to EU users after Brexit, relying on its Lithuanian license. The company obtained a restricted banking license in the UK in mid-2024, but only received full banking approval in the UK in March 2026, enabling it to conduct deposit account and lending business in its home market. Each new license enhances market trust: deposits in the EU are protected by a Lithuanian scheme, while approximately 13 million UK users now enjoy deposit insurance protection under the UK Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). Throughout its development, Revolut has consistently positioned product bundling as a core competitive advantage (i.e., a "multi-engine platform" model). The launch of a new feature often drives other businesses: for example, adding travel insurance to premium packages increases card usage; integrating cryptocurrency and stock trading within the app enhances user stickiness. By 2025, the company had 11 independent product lines, each generating over £100 million (approximately $135 million) in annual revenue, fully demonstrating the effectiveness of its feature-driven growth strategy. In its early stages, focusing on low-cost international payments and card usage scenarios allowed Revolut to identify a niche market and achieve rapid growth. The subsequent integration of fintech services such as investment, insurance, and roaming SIM cards transforms it into a "super app" with higher user stickiness, enabling cross-selling to existing users. EU and UK banking licenses translate the convenience of fintech into mainstream market-recognized trust and balance sheet management capabilities. In short, from foreign exchange cards to crypto wallets, and then to a licensed bank, each upgrade deepens user engagement on the platform and opens up new revenue engines. As of the end of 2025, Revolut has become a large-scale, profitable fintech bank. Key data for 2025 are as follows: Approximately 68.3 million individual users (up 30% year-over-year), and 767,000 enterprise clients (up 33% year-over-year); Revenue reached $6 billion (up 46% year-over-year), and pre-tax profit reached $2.3 billion (up 57% year-over-year); Profit margins were strong, with a pre-tax profit margin of 38%, higher than the previous year's 35%, demonstrating economies of scale. These figures signify that the company has achieved net profit for five consecutive years, a stark contrast to many still unprofitable private fintech companies, and also indicate that Revolut's business model is gradually acquiring the characteristics of a large-scale financial institution.

User Engagement and Product Adoption

Growth remains strong. Revolut added 16 million individual customers in 2025. More importantly, user engagement continued to deepen. The number of customers choosing Revolut as their primary bank grew by approximately 45% annually, and the amount of data used per user continued to increase: total transaction volume increased significantly by 65% ​​in 2025, reaching $1.7 trillion, and the number of transactions per user increased by 24% year-over-year.

Nearly one-third of new accounts are Revolut accounts.

Revenue Structure

The company's business is highly diversified. Total revenue in 2025 is approximately £4.5 billion, of which about half comes from transaction fees (bank card fees, foreign exchange) and subscription fees, and the remainder from investment, interest, and other financial services income.

For example, subscription revenue was approximately £708 million, bank card fees were approximately £1 billion, and wealth management and foreign exchange combined totaled approximately £1.3 billion. The corporate segment (corporate accounts) contributed approximately 16% of revenue, while credit and interest income (personal loans and credit cards) grew rapidly: credit balances reached $2.9 billion (a year-on-year increase of 120%), and interest income reached $1.3 billion. In short, Revolut's current revenue is still primarily from fees, but it is gradually shifting towards interest income to create a complementary structure.

Revenue Mix

Operating Leverage

Economies of scale are evident, and cost efficiency continues to improve. A profit margin of 38% in 2025 indicates that the growth rate of new revenue significantly exceeds the growth rate of fixed costs. Management emphasizes implementing strict cost management (e.g., allocating 90% of assets to cash and government bond investments) and adhering to technology-driven operations. Efficiency improvements are clearly evidenced: despite continued user growth, customer support response times were reduced by 40% by 2025. However, continued expansion (new markets, new licenses, and a planned £10 billion capital expenditure over five years) means overall costs will still rise. The Balance Between Maturity and Growth: Revolut currently exhibits both growth potential and scalability. The company is no longer burning cash, and profits and strong metrics demonstrate its status as a scalable financial institution. At the same time, it continues to grow rapidly (user growth exceeding 20%, with even higher growth in emerging markets) and invests heavily in new products and new regional markets. We believe it remains a growth platform but has begun to exhibit the robust characteristics of a bank. The core question is whether the company can effectively leverage its scale advantages—for example, converting users into primary account holders and advancing balance sheet products such as credit and mortgages—to maintain both growth and profit margins. Business Model and Profitability Quality Revolut's business model is based on a combination of fee/commission income, subscription fees, and interest income, with the structure becoming increasingly balanced over time. In 2025, approximately 80% of revenue was non-interest income (bank card fees, foreign exchange spreads, trading commissions, subscription fees, and wealth management service fees), and approximately 20% was net interest income (primarily from personal lending). Core Revenue Sources: Trading and Subscription Fees: Bank card payment fees (interbank clearing fees/merchant fee sharing) and foreign exchange fees remain substantial (approximately $1 billion and $800 million respectively in 2025). Paid subscription packages (Plus/Premium/Metal) are another stable source of revenue (US$708 million in 2025, a 67% year-over-year increase). This type of revenue grows in tandem with the number of customers and the scale of spending, and while it is partly cyclical (linked to consumption and travel), it also has recurring characteristics. Wealth Management Service Fees: Investment services (stock trading, ETFs, CFD spreads) generated US$876 million in revenue in 2025, a 31% year-over-year increase. Although it represents a small percentage of total revenue, it reflects an important diversification strategy—Revolut earns commissions from stock/ETF trading and cryptocurrency trading. The zero-commission ETFs launched in 2025 are expected to attract more users, but currently, wealth management product revenue still accounts for less than 20%. Interest Income (Credit Business): Revolut's credit business is still in its early stages but is growing rapidly. As of the end of 2025, the loan balance reached $2.9 billion (mainly unsecured personal loans), driving interest income of $1.3 billion, a year-on-year increase of 23%. This is the first time Revolut has achieved annual net interest income close to $1 billion, accounting for approximately 20% of total revenue. As the company transforms into a bank (mortgage loans have been launched in Lithuania, and credit products are about to be launched in the UK), interest income is expected to eventually become one of its top two revenue sources. Importantly, interest income is relatively stable, allowing the company's profits to be linked to net interest margins rather than relying on volatile fees. Corporate and SME Segment: Corporate accounts, payments, and fintech services contribute approximately 16% of revenue. This business line has lower risk (serving large enterprises with high B2B transaction volume), stronger user stickiness (stable account relationships), and has significantly increased business scale—Revolut's enterprise business processed $365 billion in transactions in 2025. Continued growth in this segment (an average of 30,000 new enterprises per month in 2025) will optimize customer diversity and fee revenue stability. Profitability Quality: This business model has shown strong profitability after scaling, but is still susceptible to cyclical fluctuations. The organic growth of high-margin revenue (bank cards, foreign exchange) and the rapid expansion of credit business have resulted in double-digit net profit margins to date. However, caution is still warranted: fee revenue is highly correlated with consumption and travel (which are cyclical), and it also made a significant contribution to profits during the cryptocurrency trading boom in 2024; as the cryptocurrency market stabilizes, past performance is partly attributable to cyclical factors. On the other hand, the ever-expanding credit ledger provides more stable revenue, while the recurring nature of subscription fees creates a "flywheel effect" as users add more services. More than 11 revenue streams demonstrate resilience: a decline in a single business line (such as reduced travel) can be partially offset by other businesses (such as credit card spending or loans). Overall, Revolut's profit structure has matured from a single fintech model to a diversified, bank-like revenue mix—still growth-oriented, but more stable than in its early stages. Revolut's core competitive advantage lies in its rich feature matrix and rapid innovation iteration capabilities. It offers a comprehensive product system within a single application—bank accounts, multi-currency wallets, real-time foreign exchange transfers, global debit cards, loans, cryptocurrency and stock trading, insurance, mobile eSIM services, and more. Localization further enhances its competitiveness: for example, Revolut offers local IBAN accounts in several EU countries, launched mortgage services in Lithuania, and even operates a mobile virtual network operator (Revolut Mobile) in the UK and Poland. This localization capability makes it more tailored to local needs than general-purpose applications. Its user interface and experience (UX/UI) are often cited by research and media as key differentiators – the extremely simple account opening process, budget management tools, real-time notifications, and peer-to-peer payment capabilities are highly attractive. Revolut's technology platform also heavily utilizes artificial intelligence and automation, such as deploying advanced AI throughout the system to improve fraud detection capabilities, achieving up to 10 times the daily fraud checks of traditional methods. This reduces human intervention internally, helping to maintain security levels during large-scale expansion. Data analytics capabilities and open APIs (such as the Revolut X API for professional crypto traders) further enhance added value. Platform Model Revolut describes itself as a "multi-engine" platform, with its advantages partly stemming from cross-selling within the platform. For example, the RevPoints points system integrates travel and payment scenarios for card spending, incentivizing continued user engagement. The 17 million RevPoints users have also boosted activity across the entire product line. Similarly, integrating cryptocurrency and stock trading within a single bank account interface significantly reduces customer acquisition costs compared to independent brokerages. A large user base (68 million) creates a distribution advantage: Revolut data shows that 63% of new users come from referrals, forming a virtuous cycle of growth. Cost Structure As a purely digital institution, Revolut's fixed operating costs are far lower than traditional banks (without physical branches), and it can iterate features more quickly. This helps to strictly control costs, supporting a high pre-tax profit margin of 38%. However, compliance and cybersecurity costs continue to rise, and the company is also investing heavily in technology and talent recruitment (reaching 7,900 employees by mid-2025). If trading volume slows, its aggressive pricing strategy (such as Revolut X's zero-fee order placement) could put pressure on the unit economics of its crypto and trading businesses. Competitive Landscape Compared to traditional banks like Barclays and Santander, Revolut's advantages lie in its agility and product breadth. The launch of its UK current account service in March 2026 makes it a direct challenger to HSBC and Barclays. Facing fintech peers like Monzo, N26, and Wise, its core advantage is its global scale and multi-business line layout: N26 and Monzo focus on a single market, while Revolut's 70 million users are spread across dozens of countries. Compared to pure crypto exchanges like Coinbase and Binance, Revolut is licensed and compliant, and deeply integrated with comprehensive financial services, making it more attractive to users who want to manage all their assets with a single application. In short, Revolut's competitive positioning is built on its channel distribution capabilities and product breadth. While it may not have a unique technological moat besides its large user community, its seamless user experience, continuous innovation, and global regulatory footprint make it difficult for any single competitor to fully match. (It should be noted that "super app" is more of a marketing term, but Revolut's product portfolio is indeed far broader than that of ordinary digital banks.) Risks, Constraints, and Unresolved Issues The rapid growth in regulatory scrutiny has attracted strict regulatory scrutiny. In April 2025, the Lithuanian bank responsible for overseeing Revolut's EU banking operations fined Revolut €3.5 million for deficiencies in its anti-money laundering system. The penalty specifically addressed "violations and deficiencies in monitoring business relationships." Similarly, UK regulators (FCA/PRA) questioned Revolut's cross-border risk management capabilities, delaying the issuance of its full UK banking license. These events demonstrate that weak compliance or mismanagement can lead to further fines or business restrictions. Unresolved issues: Can Revolut's corporate governance and anti-money laundering system keep pace with its business expansion? License Approval and Operational Implementation Risks: Obtaining each new banking license takes several years. Although it has now secured a UK license, migrating users to the new banking system will still take months. Revolut is also awaiting approval for its US banking license and is expanding in Latin America (Mexico, Peru) and Europe (applying for a French license). Delays or unforeseen conditions similar to those in 2024 could hinder growth. Operationally, adapting to new regulatory requirements (such as establishing branches and meeting local capital requirements) could weaken its speed advantage. Credit and Funding Risks: As its lending business expands, Revolut faces unforeseen credit risks. The rapidly growing $2.2 billion in unsecured loans could impact profitability if a future economic downturn leads to increased default rates. Currently, Revolut's balance sheet is conservative (90% cash/government bonds), but risks remain as mortgage and consumer credit expand. Funding sources are also crucial: most Revolut user accounts still have low balances (analysts point out that its user deposits are lower than traditional banks). To support lending with internal funding, Revolut needs to increase user engagement to attract more deposits or seek external financing channels. Profit Margin Sustainability: The current high transaction volume and rapidly growing interest income have boosted profit margins, but this pattern may come under pressure.
