Transaction volume is partly influenced by consumer spending trends; if travel decreases or the economy slows, transaction revenue may decline. Competition from traditional banks and other fintech companies is also intensifying (e.g., banks launching their own multi-currency cards). Furthermore, some of Revolut's transaction revenue—especially from cryptocurrency trading—is naturally cyclical (bull markets see significant revenue growth). A sustained bear market in crypto assets or stocks will directly impact transaction fee revenue. Management plans to rely more on lending (a more stable revenue stream) to mitigate the impact, but this model has not yet been validated at Revolut's current scale. Geographic and Market Complexity: Expanding into diverse markets such as Mexico and the Asia-Pacific region requires navigating local competition and subtle regulatory differences. The UK and EU are highly homogenized markets with high technology penetration, while consumer behavior varies significantly in other regions. Revolut's relatively uniform application model may face challenges at the cultural or banking system level (such as insurance regulations and digital identity requirements in some countries). Balancing product standardization with localization is a major constraint. Managing 70 million users across nearly 100 jurisdictions is extremely complex. Expanding customer service is a long-term issue; although customer service response time is expected to decrease by 40% by 2025, future service failures (such as prolonged system outages, fraud incidents, or unjustified account freezes in the past) could damage user trust. Maintaining a robust technology and security system is crucial. Simultaneously, venturing into crypto assets and innovative products will attract new regulatory rules (EU MiCA, UK FCA, US SEC rules). Changes in regulatory policies (such as UK stablecoin regulation and strengthened consumer protection) may force product restructuring. Product Complexity and Business Focus A rich product portfolio is an advantage, but it also carries the risk of scattered resources. Managing numerous business lines, including banking, wealth management, crypto, communications, and loyalty programs, increases complexity and disperses resources. If new businesses fail to scale, it could drag down the core banking business. The company seems to recognize this risk and has proposed incubating "new business growth points" within its diversified platform, but control at the execution level will be crucial. Digital Asset Strategy and Prospects The cryptocurrency business was launched in 2017 as a convenient one-click entry point. Revolut launched its cryptocurrency trading service at the end of 2017, allowing users to buy and sell Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin, and other tokens within the app. At the time, this feature emphasized convenience (including automatic conversion of spending) and charged a fixed low fee. By 2021, Revolut had expanded its crypto services to over 15 markets, with a significant increase in its user base (reports indicate millions of users). In 2022, the UK regulator FCA officially approved Revolut's crypto services under anti-money laundering regulations, and the company subsequently launched proof-of-stake token staking services (such as Ethereum 2.0 staking) in the UK and the European Economic Area. Compared to pure cryptocurrency exchanges, Revolut's services are simpler (users do not need to hold private keys, but simply buy and sell at specified prices through the interface) and are natively integrated with its banking applications. In the long term, crypto transaction fees are an important but volatile source of profit – Reuters noted that the "crypto boom" of 2024 significantly boosted the company's profits. However, regulatory policy changes and market cycles have led Revolut to maintain a pragmatic strategy: for example, in 2023, due to scrutiny from the US SEC, it delisted some crypto products in the US.

Current Positioning and Business Metrics

Currently, Revolut conducts its crypto trading business through two main channels: the main application for general users, and the independent advanced trading platform Revolut X for professional users. Revolut X provides a fully functional crypto exchange interface, including zero transaction fees, high liquidity, TradingView charts, and API access, and is marketed under the Revolut brand (slogan: "Crypto Exchange for Professionals"). Assets on Revolut X are held in 1:1 custody (not lent out) and are mostly stored in cold wallets. Meanwhile, the in-app crypto wallet provides instant deposit and withdrawal services for general traders.

EU users trade through Revolut Digital Assets Europe Ltd (an entity licensed by the Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission and compliant with EU MiCA regulations); UK users access crypto services through Revolut Ltd (a company that has completed FCA anti-money laundering regulatory registration).

The platform supports spot trading of approximately 250 tokens and offers staking services for a limited number of cryptocurrencies, such as Ethereum. Regarding stablecoins, Revolut has not yet launched its own branded stablecoin, but in February 2026, it joined the UK FCA regulatory sandbox to pilot GBP-backed crypto tokens, signaling its intention to enter the stablecoin business. From a business perspective, crypto services fall under the Wealth and Investment segment, alongside stocks and ETFs, and are not an independent profit center. It's worth noting that Revolut does not offer non-custodial self-custodial wallets, thus avoiding competition with hardware wallets or decentralized finance platforms, and consistently positioning itself as a "licensed crypto broker." Currently, publicly available data on Revolut's crypto business is limited; management has not disclosed the number of crypto trading users or their revenue share. However, current indications suggest that while crypto business is an important driver of user activity, it contributes relatively little to revenue: the company's major revenue streams (subscriptions, card fees, foreign exchange fees, and credit interest) all exceed $600 million, yet "cryptocurrency" is not listed as a separate item in the financial statements.

Revolut Stablecoin Monthly Trading Volume

Some indirect data suggests that crypto usage is at a moderate level: for example, the RevPoints loyalty program has 17 million users (who can earn points through crypto-related spending), while the publicly advertised Revolut X customer base represents only a fraction of that total.

Crypto trading volume is likely to fluctuate with market cycles: usage surged during the 2021-2022 bull market, while trading volume declined during the relatively calmer period of 2023-2025. The 2025 annual report highlighted a 45% year-on-year increase in main account usage and growth across almost all product lines, but did not emphasize crypto business growth, indicating that crypto was not a core growth driver last year. Revolut appears to position its crypto capabilities as a compliant distribution channel rather than a speculative side business. With a Crypto Asset Service Provider (CASP) license under the MiCA framework issued by the Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission, it became one of the first major fintech companies to comply with EU crypto regulatory requirements, enabling it to offer crypto services to retail investors across the European Economic Area. This is expected to make Revolut a one-stop digital asset aggregation platform, effectively connecting traditional financial users with the compliant crypto sector. It is reasonable to infer that the crypto business has increased Revolut's user stickiness (users completing multiple financial activities within a single application) and serves as a customer acquisition and retention tool. However, the crypto business may still represent a limited portion of total revenue. It's not a core profit driver, but rather a value-added feature that allows Revolut's product portfolio to compete with specialized fintech wallets. The crypto business does reinforce Revolut's "super app" positioning, allowing users to quickly acquire digital assets without leaving the platform, and potentially attracting younger or tech-savvy users who would otherwise use exchanges. Nevertheless, Revolut's core narrative remains centered around banking and payments. Unlike specialized exchanges, Revolut doesn't rely on crypto market making or lending (the company claims its assets are not lent out), which limits revenue but also reduces risk. The company's foray into stablecoin pilots and media reports of considering issuing its own token indicate it's exploring new payment channels, but such attempts carry regulatory and reputational risks and are currently small-scale. Looking ahead, Revolut's crypto roadmap appears to be aligned with regulatory policies and will primarily focus on incremental feature expansion. Feasible paths include: completing testing of a pound sterling stablecoin (or other token) in 2026, and considering a formal launch if regulatory approval is obtained; globally promoting Revolut X to gain more trading volume, subject to compliance with local regulations; potentially launching crypto derivatives or decentralized finance (DeFi) entry points, if regulatory permission permits; and continuously integrating crypto assets into loyalty programs or as collateral (e.g., launching crypto-secured loans in the future). Restraining factors include: constantly updated EU MiCA requirements (consumer protection, stablecoin reserves), potentially stricter stablecoin rules in the UK, and intense competition from professional crypto exchanges. For the crypto business to transition from a supplementary business to a significant segment of Revolut's operations, a major structural shift is needed, such as widespread adoption of its own stablecoin or integrating digital asset custody into everyday banking services. This requires both stringent regulatory approval and reliance on user demand. Currently, the crypto business is expected to grow steadily with the market, but its economic contribution may still be relatively limited compared to Revolut's overall banking and payments business. Key Metrics to Watch: **Personal Master Account Ratio:** The percentage of users using Revolut as their primary bank (45% year-over-year growth in 2025) – a key metric for measuring user engagement; **Customer Funds and Loan Size:** Total deposits ($67.5 billion in 2025, up 66% year-over-year) and loan size ($2.9 billion, up 120% year-over-year) – reflecting the funding base and progress towards interest income; **Revenue Growth and Structure:** Total revenue ($6 billion, up 46% year-over-year) and contributions from various segments (e.g., corporate business). 16%; Fee and Interest Income Structure); Trading Volume: Total trading volume (US$1.7 trillion in 2025, up 65% year-on-year) and breakdown of corporate and personal business (corporate business US$365 billion); Profitability: Pre-tax profit margin (38% in 2025) and return on equity – assessing operating leverage; Crypto Business Participation: Percentage of crypto trading users and crypto revenue (not publicly disclosed) – observing business investment signals through new product announcements (new tokens, staking services, etc.); Regulatory Milestones: License updates (full launch of UK business, progress of US licenses, MiCA compliance status) and compliance results (anti-money laundering audit results, etc.) This directly determines the business's growth potential.

Public Facts and Analytical Inferences

In this analysis, we strictly distinguish between publicly disclosed data and analytical inferences. For example, "68.3 million customers by the end of 2025" and "pre-tax profit of £1.7 billion" are factual data from official channels. In contrast, statements regarding the positioning of the crypto business in Revolut's strategy (such as "the crypto business is mainly for user retention and activity") are analytical interpretations based on existing clues (licenses, product integration methods, etc.), and are not information explicitly disclosed by management. In cases of insufficient evidence (such as the unknown revenue share of the crypto business), we have clearly marked the existence of uncertainty. It is important to emphasize that management's publicly expressed optimistic statements (such as the target user base of 100 million by 2027) are only considered as a strategic vision, and we will comprehensively consider them in conjunction with actual performance in our analysis. Conclusion Revolut is now a global fintech giant: no longer the travel card startup of 2015, it has grown into a diversified, large-scale banking platform. With its super-app strategy integrating payments, banking, wealth management, and crypto services, the company has achieved record profitability and massive business scale. Revolut has reached a critical turning point: transforming from a growth-driven fintech company into a mainstream financial institution (as evidenced by the UK's full banking license and achieving break-even with 70 million users). Its core strengths lie in its rich product portfolio, user loyalty, and technology-first cost structure. However, rapid expansion also brings real risks, including compliance issues such as anti-money laundering and capital regulation, as well as cyclical revenue fluctuations affected by the macro environment. While the company's digital asset strategy is clearly defined, it needs to be viewed objectively. Encryption features have strengthened the platform's competitiveness and attracted professional users, but they are only one part of its overall business strategy. Revolut's core focus remains on traditional banking: expanding deposit accounts, extending consumer credit, and consolidating its position in various markets. Therefore, crypto assets and stablecoins are beneficial for the future of finance, but not transformative factors for Revolut's core business. In short, Revolut is growing into a truly global challenger bank, and crypto-related initiatives are strategic supplements—used to enhance user engagement and future business possibilities—rather than the core of its development and transformation.

data.title: Bankless: In-depth analysis of the SEC's new framework for token classification
data.description:
Author: Jack Inabinet, Senior Analyst, Bankless; Translation: @JinseFinancexz

Last week, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) jointly released a landmark guidance document, providing a long-awaited joint guidance document that details how and when federal securities laws apply to crypto assets.

While the cryptocurrency industry has long operated in a legal gray area, this new framework attempts to replace some of the uncertainty through a token classification system to help distinguish under what circumstances digital assets qualify as securities.

Today, we will analyze this joint guidance document and speculate on how it might ultimately classify mainstream digital assets.

1. Digital Commodities

According to the SEC's interpretative guidance document, the "digital commodities" category covers crypto assets whose value derives from their "functional" role in the cryptocurrency ecosystem and supply and demand dynamics.

This broad definition has a clear intention to encompass utility tokens from numerous mature blockchain networks.

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has listed 16 crypto assets that fall into this category, as follows: Aptos (APT), Avalanche (AVAX), Bitcoin (BTC), Bitcoin Cash (BCH), Cardano (ADA), Chainlink (LINK), Dogecoin (DOGE), Ether (ETH), Hedera (HBAR), Litecoin (LTC), Polkadot (DOT), Shiba Inu (SHIB), Solana (SOL), Stellar (XLM), Tezos (XTZ), and XRP (XRP). While the SEC's guidance does not provide individual explanations for why these specific cryptocurrencies are classified as non-security digital commodities, it emphasizes that because they do not meet the legal definition of "security," they are not automatically considered securities. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) claims that its use of the term "digital commodity" is strictly based on an economic meaning to describe the typical characteristics of a class of non-security digital assets. However, the guidance document also states that any crypto asset (excluding stablecoins compliant with the GENIUS Act) may be classified as a genuine "commodity" under the Commodity Exchange Act, subjecting it to independent oversight and regulation by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC). Other popular crypto tokens not mentioned by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) but potentially qualifying as "digital commodities" include: BONK: A meme coin on the Solana network, which has evolved to integrate diverse token governance and utility functions since its inception; CC: The native utility token of the Canton blockchain, used for trading, settling transactions, synchronizing data, and transferring assets; EIGEN: A utility token based on a restaking mechanism within the EigenLayer ecosystem, used to provide economic security; ETC: A proof-of-work version of Ethereum, designated by the SEC as a non-security digital commodity; HYPE: Hyperliquid, the native fuel and staking token of the Hyperliquid network, used to pay transaction fees and ensure network security; STX: Stacks, the native fuel and staking reward token of the Stacks Bitcoin Layer 2 network, used to pay transaction fees and ensure network security; SUI: Sui Sui The network's native fuel and staking tokens are used to pay transaction fees and ensure the network's secure operation; TAO: Bittensor, the native token of the Bittensor network, used to incentivize and reward machine learning contributions in the decentralized artificial intelligence market; TRX: Tron, the native fuel and staking token of the Tron network, used to pay transaction fees and ensure the network's secure operation; XMR: Monero, the native token of the privacy-focused Monero network, used to pay transaction fees and ensure the network's secure operation.

 2、Digital Collectibles

Former Chairman of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, Gary Gensler, failed to clearly state at a congressional hearing whether tokenized Pokémon cards would be considered securities under federal law, stating that more information was needed to make a final judgment. Now, the cryptocurrency industry seems to have finally received the long-awaited answer.

A newly proposed guidance document from the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) indicates that tokenized Pokémon trading cards may be classified as "digital collectibles," but further information is required before their non-security status can be confirmed. This classification applies to crypto assets intended for collection, including those that "represent or grant" rights to artwork, music, videos, trading cards, in-game items, and previously defined meme-related rights. Similar to physical collectibles, digital collectibles do not grant holders any type of business ownership, even if they possess the right to commercially develop the underlying intellectual property. Therefore, they are naturally considered non-security. The SEC asserts that CryptoPunks, Chromie Squiggles, fan tokens, WIF, and VCOIN can all be classified as digital collectibles. While the SEC classifies memes as digital collectibles, it also states that such tokens could potentially become digital goods once they acquire "functionality" within the associated crypto ecosystem. Furthermore, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission has explicitly stated (as with physical collectibles) that most fragmented collectibles are likely to constitute securities because the buyer relies on the governing efforts of a third party. Perhaps the very necessity of physical custody of the Pokémon cards supporting the tokenized version constitutes a governing effort, thus triggering registration requirements under federal securities laws? Other popular crypto projects not mentioned by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission but currently potentially qualifying as "digital collectibles" include: Beeple's The First 5000 Days: The highest-priced NFT ever sold, its value stemming from cultural significance, artist reputation, and historical standing in the NFT market; PEPE: A meme coin inspired by internet culture, its value derived from social sentiment and virality; Pudgy Penguins: A community-centric NFT and token brand built around a penguin-themed IP, its value driven by cultural relevance; Quantum Cats: On-chain digital collectibles in the Bitcoin Ordinals series, their value based on scarcity and their position within the Bitcoin ecosystem; Saga Monkes: A limited-edition NFT series tied to the launch of the Solana Saga phone, possessing digital collectible value due to scarcity, early adopter status, and ecosystem relevance.

3. Digital Instruments

Digital instruments are cryptographic assets designed to perform specific functions. While digital instruments may possess intrinsic functional value, many are also “soul-bound,” meaning they cannot be sold and are permanently owned by a single user.

Importantly, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission states that digital instruments must not possess “inherent economic attributes or rights, such as generating passive income or granting claims on future income, profits, or assets of a business, other entity, promisor, or debtor.”

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has listed a range of use cases for digital instruments, including memberships, tickets, credentials, ownership certificates, and identity tokens. Its guidance document classifies Ethereum domain name service domains and CoinDesk's "Microcosms" NFT consensus conference tickets as digital instruments. Other digital assets that may qualify as "digital instruments" include: FanDome NFT: A collection of NFTs inspired by comic books, distributed free to participants of the DC Comics FanDome virtual conference in 2021; POAP: Digital badges generated in NFT form, minted via blockchain to prove participation in online or offline events; Propy NFT: Digital certificates representing legal ownership of physical real estate, stored on the blockchain; VeeFriends: An NFT series created by Gary Vinerchuk, allowing holders to participate in exclusive events, including the VCON conference; World ID: A soul-bound "proof of identity" certificate issued by Sam Altman's World Coin project.

4. Stablecoins

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission's interpretative guidance on non-security stablecoins intentionally maintains a concise and direct style, clearly stating that after it takes effect in January next year, it will treat payment-type stablecoins that conform to the description of the GENIUS Act as non-security.

Therefore, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission itself will not impose registration requirements on payment-type stablecoins that conform to the GENIUS Act during the issuance and redemption process (although these stablecoins themselves will still be subject to extensive supervision by other agencies within the U.S. regulatory system).

However, the guidance document clarifies that prior to the enactment of the GENIUS Act, previously defined "covered stablecoins" were still considered non-securities outside the agency's jurisdiction. For a stablecoin to meet this definition, its holders must not have the right to "receive any interest, profits, or other returns," and its reserves must consist entirely of "US dollars and/or other assets considered low-risk and readily liquid." While the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) does not specifically designate which stablecoins are considered non-securities, instruments pegged to the U.S. dollar that appear to fit its definition of "covered stablecoins" include: PYUSD: A stablecoin issued by Paxos Trust Company, a federally registered national trust bank, for PayPal; USAT: Tether's latest stablecoin pegged to the U.S. dollar, designed to comply with the GENIUS Act; USDC: A stablecoin issued by Circle, over-collateralized 1:1 with U.S. dollars and risk-free U.S. dollar investments; KlarnaUSD: A stablecoin sponsored by Klarna, a buy-now-pay-later giant, and issued by Stripe's Bridge.

5. Digital Securities

It can be said that the most influential guidance document from the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is precisely its most ambiguous part.

The regulator maintains that any digital asset that fails the Howe test is considered a security, a determination that has historically involved difficult legal disputes.

The SEC also recognizes that encapsulated versions of financial instruments that are explicitly defined as securities under federal law (such as tokenized money market funds, stocks, and other types of structured investment products) are digital securities.

However, this does not mean that all "encapsulated" tokens are securities.

The guidance document explains that protocol mining, protocol staking, and the "packaging" of non-security crypto assets do not constitute the issuance or sale of securities. The document further clarifies that "certain" airdrops do not constitute "fund investment" under the Howe Test. However, the agency cannot provide a definitive answer as to when a digital asset becomes a digital security. In each case, determining whether an investment contract (i.e., a security) has been issued or sold must be based on the specific facts and circumstances of how the issuer marketed and promoted its asset. If a non-security crypto asset is issued through an investment contract that meets the Howe Test criteria, it will become a security. Furthermore, the initial issuance of a non-security crypto asset through an investment contract does not imply that it is permanently a security; once the original investment contract is fulfilled, or the issuer fails to fulfill its committed operational efforts, the crypto asset can become a non-security. While the SEC refuses to list examples of assets it deems securities, it is noteworthy that its guidance document classifies XRP as a non-security "digital commodity," although courts ultimately ruled that certain sales of the token constituted investment contracts that violated federal securities laws. While this is a step towards regulatory clarity, this aspect of the SEC guidance ultimately reinforces rather than resolves ambiguity. It merely extends existing federal securities laws to the digital asset space, while reigniting market concerns about projects that promise unfinished work or future functionality. Digital goods, digital collectibles, digital instruments, and stablecoins may be non-securities in nature, but they can still be issued or sold through investment contracts, which would subject issuers to federal securities laws.

data.title: x402: Why are Google, Cloudflare, Stripe, and others entering the market?
data.description:
Source: PayAI Network; Compiled by: Jinse Finance Claw

In just over a year, the x402 protocol has transformed from an open-source experiment into a standard supported by tech and payment giants.

This article will delve into who is using x402 for development, why its adoption is accelerating, and what this means for developers and builders positioning themselves in the "Agentic Economy."

Core Summary

  • x402 has strong backing: Google, Cloudflare, Stripe, Visa, Amazon Web Services (AWS), and Circle have all taken action to support or integrate the x402 protocol, signifying that it is no longer in the experimental stage.

  • Formal Foundation Established: Cloudflare and Coinbase jointly established the x402 Foundation in September 2025, with founding members spanning the cloud computing, payments, and infrastructure sectors.

  • AI as a Catalyst: The intelligent agent AI market is projected to grow from $7.3 billion in 2025 to $324 billion in 2034, creating huge demand for “Machine-Native Payment Rails.”

  • Solana Leads Settlement: With fees of less than 1 cent and confirmation speeds of 400 milliseconds, Solana has become the dominant blockchain for high-frequency x402 micropayments.

  • Start Building Now: Open-source SDKs and Facilitator infrastructure, including the Solana-first toolkit from PayAI Network, allow developers to start monetizing their APIs and AI services now.

x402 Adoption is Accelerating: Who's Developing With It

When Coinbase officially released the x402 protocol specification in May 2025, it activated the long-dormant HTTP 402 (Required Payment) status code for stablecoin payments directly embedded in web requests. Within just a few months, its adoption had expanded far beyond the crypto-native community.

By September 2025, Cloudflare and Coinbase co-founded the x402 Foundation, with Google, Visa, AWS, and Circle joining as founding members. Smart agent payments are no longer a future concept; they have now become a cross-industry alliance spanning cloud computing, global payments, and internet infrastructure.

What distinguishes this moment from previous crypto payment attempts is the **convergence**: infrastructure providers, payment processors, and AI platforms are all converging on x402. The internet's payment layer is being restructured for the smart agent era, and the giants are choosing to build it together.

Google and x402: The Search Giant Enters the Internet-Native Payments Market

Google's involvement with x402 goes far beyond simply displaying its logo on the foundation's page. The company has partnered with Cloudflare, Accenture, PayPal, and MetaMask to develop the Agent Payments Protocol (AP2). AP2 positions x402 as the underlying settlement mechanism for agent-initiated purchases and machine-to-machine transactions running on Google Cloud infrastructure.

This is crucial for two reasons: First, Google Cloud is one of the largest platforms for deploying AI workloads, and Gemini-powered agents are becoming increasingly autonomous. These agents need a way to pay for external services (such as data, computing power, and API calls) without human intervention. x402 provides just that: a payment handshake that occurs within a standard HTTP request.

Google's joining Visa, AWS, and Circle in the x402 Foundation sends a clear signal to enterprise buyers: intelligent agent business systems are not just a game for the crypto industry; these companies plan to provide production-grade infrastructure that can collectively serve billions of users. Cloudflare and x402: Payments at the Edge Cloudflare's adoption of x402 is perhaps the most architecturally significant move in the ecosystem. The company launched a **"Pay-per-crawl"** feature, allowing website owners to charge AI bots and crawlers for content access via stablecoin micropayments, turning security vulnerabilities into revenue streams. Since Cloudflare exists as a reverse proxy at the front end of millions of websites, embedding x402 payment support at the network edge means that any site using Cloudflare can monetize its traffic without changing its application code. For developers building applications on Workers or Pages, this eliminates significant friction. Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince has publicly stated that x402 has the potential to become the foundational internet protocol for machine-to-machine payments, comparing its trajectory to HTTP and HTTPS. When a non-crypto-native infrastructure company with such a massive reach considers payment primitives a top-tier feature, it represents a paradigm shift. Stripe and x402: Traditional Payments Meet a New Standard Stripe's move towards x402 is arguably the strongest confirmation signal for mainstream adoption. The company launched a preview of machine payments integrated with x402, enabling AI agents to use USDC payment API calls and digital services, with payments settled in fiat currency to the merchant's Stripe balance. This "bridging" role is crucial. Stripe processed over $1 trillion in payments in 2024 and is the payment backbone for millions of internet businesses. Its support for x402 means that existing merchants can access stablecoin-based micropayments without rebuilding their payment stack or learning crypto infrastructure. This is a pragmatic entry point for the millions of SaaS platforms, marketplaces, and API providers already using Stripe. They can accept x402 payments from AI agents while continuing to manage their revenue through the familiar Stripe control panel. This is exactly how the protocol scales: by connecting to it where the business is located. AWS Deepens its Commitment to Agent Payments: Integrating x402 into its Core Services To avoid being overtaken by cloud competitors, Amazon Web Services (AWS) has significantly expanded its commitment to the agent economy by integrating x402 support into its Lambda and API Gateway services. In a recent post, AWS announced that developers can now natively configure API Gateway to return 402 status codes for unauthenticated or unpaid requests and leverage AWS Lambda to handle x402 payment negotiation and settlement through the PayAI Network Facilitator. This move significantly reduces friction for AWS's millions of developers monetizing their serverless functions and data streams, positioning x402 as a first-tier feature of their pay-as-you-go cloud service. As a founding member of the x402 Foundation, AWS is providing production-grade tools to ensure that agents running on platforms like Amazon Bedrock can seamlessly pay for resources across the entire cloud ecosystem.

data.title: From Free to Paid: Polymarket's Paid Transition and Growth Flywheel
data.description:
Author: locha, co-founder of Fuul; Translator: xz Jinse Finance

Polymarket's trading volume reached $9.55 billion last month, but generated almost no revenue. This will change on March 30th: the platform will launch charging fees across all ten market categories. The question worth exploring is not whether prediction markets are truly viable, but how Polymarket got to where it is today.

1. Problem: How to launch a market that no one is using

Prediction markets face a severe cold start problem. Without liquidity, traders won't enter; without traders, liquidity providers won't enter—and both sides need to trust the market after it demonstrates accuracy. Accuracy requires trading volume, and trading volume requires the participation of both parties.

Two years ago, the entire prediction market industry had only about 4,000 monthly active users and an annual trading volume of $15.8 billion. Today, Polymarket alone has a monthly trading volume of $9.55 billion. The industry's total trading volume is projected to reach $63.5 billion in 2025, a fourfold increase year-over-year. In February 2026, Polymarket and Kalshi's combined monthly trading volume reached $16.8 billion, pushing the industry's annualized trading volume towards $200 billion. How did they grow from 4,000 users to this scale? The answer isn't just better products, but also incentives.

2、Core Concept: Adopting Mature Strategies of Cryptocurrency Exchanges

Polymarket did not create a new incentive model, but directly adopted the growth primitives that cryptocurrency exchanges have already proven at a trillion-dollar scale.

  • Referral Programs: Binance, OKX, and Bybit have all built massive user bases through referral commission mechanisms, offering 10%-50% of trading fees to any user who brings in a new trader. Binance used this to expand to 280 million users and $20 billion in daily trading volume. These programs turn every user into a distribution channel.

  • Builder Code: Hyperliquid pioneered this model in the DeFi space: opening up APIs to developers, enabling them to route orders, attribute trading volume, and earn fees. In July 2025, the daily revenue of builder code on Hyperliquid exceeded $10 million, with applications like pvp[.]trade and Axiom driving a large volume of trading through their own interfaces. Market Maker Rebates: All major exchanges redistribute a portion of their order book fees to market makers. Subsidizing liquidity providers to narrow spreads and increase trading volume is a classic technique in exchange design. Polymarket was the first prediction market to simultaneously run all three mechanisms. However, they did not launch all mechanisms at once, but implemented them in phases over four years, with each phase addressing a specific bottleneck issue.

    Phase 1: Starting Liquidity from Zero (2022-2023)

    In early 2022, Polymarket launched a cycle-based liquidity mining program: with a 7-day cycle, approximately 50,000 USDC and 10,000 UMA were distributed to all users providing liquidity in each cycle. Rewards were calculated per block to ensure the order book remained active 24/7.

    ... By March 2023, the program was upgraded to a formal market maker rewards program, distributing rewards daily based on spread tightness and order book depth. By early 2025, the estimated cumulative subsidies had reached tens of millions of USDC. The goal is simple: to incentivize people to create liquidity, making the product available. Without liquidity, there is no price discovery; without price discovery, the product cannot exist. Phase Two: Capturing Event-Driven Demand Through Aggressive Subsidies (Late 2023 - Early 2025) Polymarket has invested over $10 million in direct USDC incentives across hundreds of markets, building upon the core market maker program with accelerated incentives for specific events. Temporary high-yield pools and volume-based rebates are available for elections, cryptocurrency cycles, and major geopolitical events. This is pure customer acquisition spending, supported by a treasury. While costly, the timing is precise. The 2024 US presidential election will bring prediction markets into the mainstream. In November 2024 alone, Polymarket attracted nearly 300,000 users to participate in betting, totaling over $2.5 billion. Polymarket's ability to offer the deepest order book depth is due to its two-year commitment to subsidizing liquidity. Phase Three: Introducing a Yield Mechanism to Retain Funds (September 2025) Attracting traders to deposit funds is one issue; keeping them on the platform between events is another. Holding Rewards will launch on September 24, 2025: providing an annualized 4% USDC yield on open positions in selected markets, paid out daily. Sampling positions hourly means rewards are linked to the persistence of holdings, not just trading activity. Polymarket borrowed a fundamental DeFi model (deposit rewards) and applied it to prediction markets. Around the same time, an initial version of the referral program quietly launched: new users who deposited funds through a referral code received a 10 USDC credit reward. This simple, low-friction entry method aimed to lower the cold start barrier for individual users, not just the market itself. Phase Four: Launching the Fee Engine (January-March 2026) At this point, the model began to become self-sustaining. January 5, 2026: First, taker fees were implemented in the 15-minute cryptocurrency prediction market (peaking at 1.56% with a 50% probability). A market maker rebate program was launched simultaneously, funded by these fees. The key shift was that takers now provided funding for market makers. Liquidity is no longer subsidized by the treasury. Mid-February: Fee and rebate mechanisms expanded to sports (college basketball, Serie A) and shorter-cycle cryptocurrency markets. February 17: Sponsored liquidity rewards opened to all users. Any user can deposit USDC into a smart contract that automatically rewards liquidity providers in any market of their choice. This democratizes the incentive mechanism. Hedge funds, institutions, and retail investors can now launch liquidity in niche markets without Polymarket involvement. March 6: Fees fully covered all cryptocurrency markets (1-hour, 4-hour, daily, weekly). March 17-19: Over $2 million in targeted liquidity rewards provided for "March Madness". Each NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament match offers a $100,000 prize pool, with an estimated $6 million to be awarded throughout the tournament. **March 23-24:** Two measures are implemented simultaneously. Market maker rebates are expanded to almost all new markets. The full referral program is open to all traders with a lifetime trading volume exceeding $10,000: 30% commission on takers' fees from direct referrals and 10% commission on second-level referrals, valid for 180 days and continuing until December 2027. **March 30:** Fee markets expand from two categories to ten. Categories such as Politics, Finance, Economics, Culture, Weather, and Technology begin generating revenue. The Builder Code program continues to operate during this period, providing developers with an API to route orders, attribute trading volume, and earn weekly USDC rewards. Even before the referral program even launches, aggregators like Dome (acquired by Polymarket in early 2026) and open-source tools like pmxt are already built on this infrastructure. The Builder Code program continues to run during this period, providing developers with APIs to route orders, attribute trade volume, and earn weekly USDC rewards. Even before the referral program even launches, aggregators like Dome (acquired by Polymarket in early 2026) and open-source tools like pmxt are already built on this infrastructure. 3. No points, no tasks, no tokens. Only USDC. Every incentive running on Polymarket is paid in USDC. Liquidity rewards, market maker rebates, holding rewards, referral commissions—all settled in the same stablecoin traders use for trading. This is a well-considered choice. This means that each incentive has a clear, immediately visible cost and a measurable return on investment. This means Polymarket can calculate its unit economics like a real business, rather than a growth experiment relying on token subsidies. It also means that the transition from treasury-funded subsidies to fee-funded incentives is clear and smooth: the same currency, the same mechanism, different funding sources.

    From "paying people to participate" to "using fees to keep people engaged," this complete path took four years. Most cryptocurrency projects have never completed this transition. Polymarket did.

    4. Current Data Overview (and why this is the bottom line, not the top line)

    So what does all this mean? Castle Labs did the calculations: taking the transaction volume of each category over the most recent 30 days, applying the new fee structure, and estimating revenue.

    This is a benchmark, not a prediction. These markets have already reached such trading volumes before fees were introduced and referral programs were launched. The incentive flywheel hasn't even started turning yet. But the following data illustrates its conservatism: the referral program just launched on March 23rd. For the first time, every opinion leader, key opinion leader, media company, and content creator in the cryptocurrency space has a direct financial incentive to drive users to Polymarket: 30% of the taker's commission for direct referrals and 10% for second-level referrals, paid daily in USDC. This is the same mechanism that previously transformed crypto Twitter, YouTube, and trading academies into distribution machines for Binance and Bybit. The only difference is that the product is no longer a leveraged perpetual contract exchange, but something that people outside the crypto space would genuinely want to use. Imagine what might happen next: a political podcast releasing a Polymarket referral link during election season; a sports media account including a referral code when embedding market odds predictions; a financial news email linking to an economic forecasting market. Now, these promoters earn real revenue from every trader they bring in. The referral program will convert Polymarket's entire audience into a paid sales team, and the potential user base of prediction markets is orders of magnitude larger than that of cryptocurrency trading.

    5. Conclusion

    The cryptocurrency industry has spent over a decade answering the question, "What's the point?" Trading and speculation were the first undisputed answer. Stablecoins followed closely. Prediction markets are becoming the next answer.

    The launch of fees on March 30th is not a trivial footnote; it's setting off a super-fast growth flywheel.

Author: Edward Zitron, Compiled: Block unicorn

Foreword

The entire AI bubble is built on a

While all of this is very strange and suspicious, I must be clear: there is currently no definitive answer to explain all of this except that NVIDIA GPUs did indeed somehow flow into China. I believe it's impossible that Jensen Huang was completely unaware of this, and it's almost impossible for multi-billion dollar GPUs to appear in places where NVIDIA was unaware.

In short, Supermicro CEO Charles Liang has not yet commented on Wally Liaw's comments.

In short, Supermicro CEO Charles Liang has not yet commented on Wally Liaw's comments.

Or comments from their alleged accomplices, with the company only issuing a statement saying their actions “violated the company’s policies and compliance controls.” It seems no one has asked Jensen Huang if he knew about this—neither Megaspeed, Supermicro, nor anyone in the past few years has asked him any challenging questions. However, Huang stated in May 2025 that “there is no evidence of any AI chip transfer,” and that the countries involved “will be very careful in their self-monitoring.” For legal reasons, I must speak very cautiously: I cannot say Huang did anything wrong or lied, but I find it unbelievable, even absurd, that he was completely unaware of this. Really? According to a December 2025 report in *The Information*, hundreds of millions, even billions of dollars worth of GPUs are flowing into China, and Huang was completely unaware? I think it's highly unlikely, though of course, I can't be entirely sure. If Nvidia really knew—of course, I'm not saying they actually knew—it's an absolute huge scandal, but aside from a few brave individuals at The Information and Bloomberg who genuinely care about the truth, almost no one's paying attention. Has anyone asked Jensen Huang? He's a frequent face on camera. **P.S.:** Earlier today, Senators Jim Banks and Elizabeth Warren wrote to Trump's Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, requesting the Commerce Department take "all necessary and appropriate measures" to prevent Nvidia chips from flowing to China, including a possible ban on exports to countries considered intermediaries such as Malaysia, Thailand, Vietnam, and Singapore. Liaw's arrest seems to have caused some controversy in Washington, and I wouldn't be surprised if Jensen Huang testifies before Congress at some point. **I'd also like to add that what shocks me is how many people are dismissing Supermicro and moving on.** But it's a huge joke that the major providers of the two mainstream cloud platforms (Crusoe and CoreWeave) and a smaller cloud platform (Lambda, to which they also rent cloud capacity) are unaware that several percentage points of their revenue are flowing directly to China through one of their co-founders. I hope we can eventually find out the truth. Nevertheless, this despicable tactic almost exposes the desperation of everyone involved. The End of Software Engineering: Hyperscale Data Center Operators Force Employees to Use AI, Leading to Degraded Product Quality and Service Crashes. So, I want to explain something very clearly to you, because you need to understand how bad things are: Hyperscale data center operators are forcing everyone in the company to use AI tools as much as possible, tying salaries and performance to token consumption, and actively encouraging non-technical personnel to write features actually for production use. In reality, this means everyone is expected to spend all day fiddling with AI tools and consuming large amounts of tokens, and for designers at some companies, they're even expected to actively write features without any coding knowledge. "How did I know the last part? Because a reliable source told me—I'll stop here." People might mistakenly believe this signifies a leap forward in AI efficiency, but the reality is a host of incomplete internal dashboards that spend hours measuring random user data or converting files just to theoretically save a few minutes. While non-technical personnel aren't necessarily allowed to deploy code directly to production, their poorly written pseudo-software—written without any technical knowledge—is then "fixed" by real software engineers who are equally responsible for fulfilling their duties. These tools also allow mediocre software engineers to cause more damage than ever before. At least in one hyperscale data center, LLM usage was relatively unrestricted (even actively incentivized), with almost anyone able to launch their own OpenClaw "AI agent" (actually a series of LLMs claiming to perform various operations on your inbox or Slack, but offering no apparent benefit other than deleting all your emails). In Meta's case, this ultimately led to a serious security breach. According to internal Meta communications and incident reports seen by *The Information*, a major security alert occurred last week. A Meta software engineer used an internal intelligent agent tool similar to OpenClaw to analyze a technical issue posted by another Meta employee on an internal forum. Internal communications state that after the analysis, the AI ​​agent responded to the original question on the forum and offered advice on the technical issue. All of this was done without the employee's authorization. According to *The Information*, Meta systems store a large amount of company and user-related data, which unauthorized engineers could access. The incident was marked as a sec-1 incident, the second highest level in Meta's internal ranking system for security incidents. This incident followed multiple issues with Amazon's Kiro and Q LLM systems. The following is a report by Eugene King of *Business Insider*: March 2 On March 5th, another system glitch caused a 99% drop in orders on Amazon's North American marketplace, resulting in the loss of 6.3 million orders. An internal document revealed that Amazon's AI tool Q was one of the main causes of the incident. LLM is destroying Big Tech from within. Despite the overwhelming (and exhausting) marketing campaigns surrounding the "power of AI code," I believe these incidents are just the beginning of the real consequences of AI coding tools: the slow disintegration of the tech industry's software stack. Even the most incompetent idiot can be mimicked by an LLM engineer. I mean, you can tell it, "Write me some software that can do this" or "Look at this code and fix it," and the LLM will keep saying, "You can do it" and "That's a great solution." The problem is, while an LLM can write "all" code, that doesn't mean the code is good, or that someone can read it and understand its intent (because these models don't think), or that having a lot of code is a good thing now and in the future for any company built using generative code. LLM-based code is often verbose and rarely conforms to internal coding guidelines and standards, guaranteeing a longer reading time. This naturally means that those responsible for reviewing it either just skim it or hand it over to another LLM to figure out exactly what to do. Worse still, the use of LLMs is completely directionless. Why would people at Meta use it? OpenClaw? Besides burning through a huge amount of tokens, what's the point of OpenClaw? Consider this: You explicitly instruct all engineers in your company to write code using LLM tokens, and incentivize them to do so by tracking LLM usage. Now, you've significantly increased your company's operating costs (costs consumed through tokens) and code creation. To be clear, letting LLM write all your code means you stop writing code, stop learning how to code, and won't become a better software engineer as a result. This means that almost all large tech companies are encouraging software engineers to stop learning how to write software or solve software architecture problems. If you're just someone who looks at code, your skill level depends on the quality of the code generated by the model. As Mo Bitar... As discussed recently, these models are designed to motivate, confuse, and tell you how great you are, when you only glance at a bunch of rewritten code that, even if it works, eventually evolves into a single entity whose purpose and intent are merely code generated by the model based on your prompts. Worse still, mega-corporations like Meta and Amazon are keen on laying off thousands at a time, making it harder to understand why things were built the way they were. This becomes even more complicated when an LLM built with a lack of thought and intent. Many trillion-dollar companies have code written with these things, and the writers (engineers and non-engineers alike!) may be gone a month or a year later, unable to explain what prompts they used. We've already seen the consequences! Amazon lost hundreds of thousands of orders! Meta... A major security breach has occurred! The foundations of these companies are being eroded by millions of lines of poorly written code, code that at best occasionally earns the approval of someone whose resume says "software engineer"—and who is frequently fired. This increases the likelihood that someone who truly understands the situation or knows why something is built that way can prevent things from going wrong. Remember: Google, Amazon, Microsoft, and Meta all possess vast amounts of personal information, private conversations, critical legal documents, financial information, and in some cases even Social Security numbers. These four companies, and a worrying large portion of the tech industry, are actively encouraging their software engineers to disregard the software itself. Oh, you're coding much faster with AI? What does that actually mean? What exactly did you build? Do you understand how it works? Did you review the code before it went live, or did you assume it was fine just because it didn't have any errors? This is creating a biblical plague in the field of software engineering—the entire tech industry is built on massive amounts of unmanageable and unintentional code driven by managers and executives who are not doing their jobs. LLM allows the incompetent to masquerade as capable, inefficient individuals to produce work-related outputs stemming from a dislike of labor and craft, and fosters the malpractices of Silicon Valley's mediocrity. Everything in Silicon Valley seems to revolve around growth; "more" is often equated with "more valuable." Kevin Roose of The New York Times—in a strikingly journalistic style—recently wrote an article praising the competition within Silicon Valley to burn ever more tokens using AI models: An OpenAI engineer last week processed 210 billion "tokens" (enough to fill Wikipedia 33 times) using the company's AI model, the highest processing volume among all employees. Anthropic's AI coding system, Claude Code, generated over $150,000 in fees within a month. At tech companies like Meta and Shopify, managers have begun incorporating AI usage into performance reviews, rewarding employees who heavily utilize AI tools and criticizing those who don't. This has become the new normal for programmers, among the first white-collar workers to feel the impact of AI on the economy. AI was originally intended to help tech companies increase productivity and reduce costs. But it has also spawned a costly new identity game known as "token maximization," where AI-obsessed employees are eager to prove their productivity. Roose explains that Meta and OpenAI both have internal leaderboards showing how many tokens you've used; one software engineer in Stockholm "spent more on tokens than his salary," though Roose adds that these tokens... It was paid for by the company. Roose described a morbid corporate culture where OpenAI rewards those who spend huge sums on tokens. He added that he had spoken with several tech workers who spent thousands of dollars a day buying tokens "just to show off." Roose added an even more bizarre detail: someone exploited a vulnerability in Claude's $20 monthly subscription using software developed by Figma, burning through $70,000 worth of tokens. Despite all this spending, Roose struggled to find anyone who could explain what they were doing besides "using parallel-running coding agents to maintain large, complex software," but he eventually found one particularly useful piece of information—it might all be a performance: They generally believe that AI coding tools have improved their productivity. However, some see the use of AI as a strategic move—to demonstrate to colleagues and bosses that they are up-to-date, as the era of manual coding seems to be coming to an end. I do give Roose some credit, because he questions, "...whether these token maximizers are producing anything good, or are they just wasting their time churning out useless code to try and appear busy?" That said, I still find this story chilling and dangerously close to the behavior of drug addicts and cult members. In this report published in one of the world's largest-circulation newspapers, Roose couldn't find a single "token maximizer" who could truly describe their work, consistent with my past experiences assessing those who incessantly talk about the power of "proxy coding." These people are sick; they are participating in a wicked, toxic culture based on unnecessary spending and endless consumption. Companies that encourage users to burn tokens are actually creating a culture of productivity through waste and fostering a destructive tendency—one stemming from users constantly looking for things to do rather than doing things purposefully. In their pursuit of so-called "artificial intelligence," simply because other companies are doing it, they are destined to make their software crudely written and poorly maintained. Anyone who has actually worked knows that those who appear most efficient are often the most useless, because everything they do is to appear efficient, not to produce any truly valuable results. A recent Business Insider interview with someone who was laid off from Amazon after learning about "artificial intelligence" and "atmosphere coding" is a good example. He was surprised to find that these so-called skills did not save him from being laid off: "During the October layoffs, everyone was discussing whether artificial intelligence was a reason for the layoffs." At the time, the company encouraged us to use artificial intelligence, but I didn't think it took my job. I was responsible for writing internal product descriptions at Amazon, and when using AI, I needed it to remove some irrelevant words to make it sound like a human. Despite my concerns, I used AI, but in my opinion, it was far from replacing my job. Before being laid off, I participated in the AI ​​development of Amazon's internal website. I had never really touched programming before, but with the help of a colleague, I learned how to code through trial and error. I felt that using AI in this project and showcasing my different skills would make me more valuable, but ultimately, it didn't save me from being laid off. To be clear, this person was a victim. Amazon forced him to learn useless skills, to create useless things in an expensive and inefficient way, and despite being pressured to use tools he didn't like, he ultimately lost his job. Note: If you read this and think she should make better use of artificial intelligence, you've been fooled. You're being tricked into doing unpaid marketing work for AI companies that actually hate you. This person was actively involved in the AI ​​development of Amazon's internal website and had to "learn Vibe Code through a lot of trial and error and with the help of colleagues." Was it worth it for her? Was it worth it for her colleagues? No! In fact, in all these damn AI coding hype Twitter accounts and endless pronouncements about the powerful capabilities of AI agents, I find almost no record of anything substantial other than someone saying, "Yeah, I seem to be more efficient." I'm convinced that in the near future, some major tech service will suffer from an unfixable glitch caused by thousands of people using AI coding tools to develop software, compounded by layoffs and a corporate culture that encourages employees to pretend to be busy rather than actually create useful things, further exacerbating the talent drain. What else can you expect? You give people a number they can improve, a number that makes them look like they're doing a better job, and what do you think they'll do? Try to increase their efficiency? Or make the most of those numbers, even if it's not necessary? I haven't even talked about how expensive all this actually is, partly because it's hard to fully grasp. But what I do know is that big tech companies are putting themselves through crisis after crisis, especially when Anthropic and OpenAI stopped subsidizing their models in ways that allowed people to spend $2,500 or more on a $200 monthly subscription. What about those who rely on these models? What about those who have forgotten how to work because they've let AI write all the code? Will they still be able to work? Large language models are creating Silicon Valley's Habsburg families—employees who become intellectually trapped once they start relying on them. These models are so heavily subsidized that their bosses encourage them to use them as much as possible. While they might be able to return to the workforce, a software engineer who has only used a large language model for more than a few months will have to relearn basic work habits and find their skills limited to the last training version of the model they recently used. I believe there are many software engineers who use these models ethically; they read all the code, have a complete understanding of the industry, and use it as a means to handle specific units of work they have complete control over. I'm also sure there are some who simply get the program to do something, glance at the code, and release it. We can't count the number of people in either category, but hearing Spotify's CEO say that their top developers basically stop writing code deeply worries me because this doesn't replace software engineering at all—it's just blindly eliminating friction and imposing "good" or "correct" standards on users, and Spotify... However, this is deliberately exaggerated. Ultimately, this era tests a person's ability to understand and appreciate friction. Friction can sometimes be a good thing. When I encounter problems I don't understand, I try my best to figure them out, and once I grasp them, the feeling is simply wonderful. Over the past three years, I've had to self-study a great deal about finance, accounting, and the broader technology industry, and I've been frustrated countless times by things I couldn't understand, even doubting whether I could truly comprehend certain things. I have plenty of time, but unfortunately, many software engineers face increasingly insane deadlines set by bosses who know nothing about software development, let alone the capabilities of LLM or what responsible software engineering is. The pressure from superiors to use these models simply because they are "faster than humans writing code" is a disastrous confusion between "fast" and "good," all stemming from the hype surrounding LLMs peddled by venture capitalists and the media. The myth that "any code can be written" is untenable. Generative code is a digital ecosystem disaster, and because companies are required to write as much code as possible as quickly as possible, this disaster will take years to repair. Everyone involved must be held accountable, especially those poorly managed software companies, who will ultimately suffer the disastrous consequences of building software on sand. The tech industry has poisoned itself with the lies of AI. Ultimately, everything about AI is built on lies. Hundreds of gigabytes of data centers under development are actually equivalent to only 5 gigabytes under construction. Hundreds of billions of dollars in GPU sales are mostly idle, waiting to be used. Anthropic's consistently high annualized revenue eventually reached $5 billion within four years, while its salaries and computing expenses totaled $25 billion or more. Despite the sheer number of data centers reportedly being built, it seems no one is profiting from renting out AI computing resources. AI's supposed ability to "write all code" actually means that all large software companies are churning out junk code in their codebases while operating costs are skyrocketing. Software engineers haven't been replaced—they've been laid off because the software designed to replace them is too expensive, not because they've actually replaced anyone. A little deeper into the industry reveals that we are witnessing one of the worst business failures in history. Those self-satisfied, condescending AI advocates exist solely to blind you to the harsh truth—AI... It generates almost no benefit, lacks tangible productivity gains, and in the long run, it seems likely to harm the productivity and efficiency of the employees forced to use it. Any executive who forces employees to use AI is a cold-blooded idiot who has no idea what real work is like and is likely a lazy, selfish moron. Everyone I've met at large tech companies is frustrated; they're constantly pressured to "embrace AI," launch more products, and do more things, but there's no real definition of what "more" actually means or what it contributes to the overall picture. Meanwhile, they're constantly worried about being laid off because a very harmful corporate culture is brewing around these services. It's poison for the future of the tech industry. It's costly, inefficient, and will damage users' learning abilities and work efficiency, depriving them of opportunities to learn and grow, hindering their progress, and ultimately leading them to mechanically repeat instructions, lacking knowledge and declining skills. Those who cheer for artificial intelligence are either ignorant, cowardly, bribed, cunning, or eager to be pioneers of the next era—even if that era is terrible, even if it's illogical, even if a little thought reveals that it's simply impossible. Ultimately, AI is a test of your introspection. Can you tell when you truly understand something? Can you explain why you believe something, and not just because someone tells you to believe it, or because you feel guilty for holding a different view? Do you genuinely want to know the truth, or do you merely possess the ability to access information when needed? How much joy do you derive from becoming a better person? If you can't answer that question definitively, perhaps you should just use LLM, because you don't really care about anything. Ultimately, you're the perfect sucker for the AI ​​industry, which can't sell itself without fabricating lies to market what it can (or theoretically can) do.
